  • 302 for permanent apex→www — should be 301
Production example — Flipkart

Context: http→https→www redirect chain historically hurt sale TTFB — consolidated to single 301 at edge.

Why they chose this approach: One-hop redirect at CDN; 301 for permanent apex→www.

  • Pros: Saves 200–400ms RTT per redirect hop
  • Pros: Browser caches 301 — repeat visits faster
  • Pros: Cleaner analytics — single canonical URL
  • Cons: Wrong 302 on permanent move repeats cost every visit
  • Cons: OAuth redirect URI must match canonical exactly
  • Cons: Regional TLDs (flipkart.com vs app) need careful rules
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • 307 vs 302? 307 preserves method — important for POST not becoming GET.
  • OAuth redirect URIs? Exact match required — trailing slash matters.
Metrics to mention
  • Redirect hop count in Lighthouse
  • TTFB including redirects

Closing: Fix at edge: one hop apex→canonical https URL.

18. Download file vs render HTML in browser
How to open (30 sec)

Content-Disposition and MIME drive behavior — plus client-generated downloads.

Timeline — draw left-to-right while speaking (5 min)
  1. Response headers — Content-Type + Content-Disposition: attachment vs inline.
  2. Browser choice — PDF may inline with plugin; CSV often downloads; sniffing restricted.
  3. Client-generated — Blob + object URL + programmatic anchor click + revokeObjectURL.
  4. Streaming — Large exports via ReadableStream — avoid holding entire file in RAM.
Deep dive (interviewer picks one)

Security: Never trust client filename alone — server sets Content-Disposition filename* UTF-8.

Verify in DevTools
  • Network headers on download response
  • Memory when generating large Blob — watch tab crash
Pitfalls & trade-offs
  • Giant JSON.stringify on main thread before download
  • Missing revokeObjectURL → memory leak
Production example — Amazon

Context: Order invoice PDF — Content-Disposition attachment; seller reports CSV via async job + Blob download in browser.

Why they chose this approach: Stream large CSV with ReadableStream; revokeObjectURL after click.

  • Pros: User gets file without navigation away from seller dashboard
  • Pros: Async job avoids timeout on 1M row export
  • Pros: Correct MIME from server prevents XSS via download
  • Cons: Huge Blob on main thread OOM on old laptops
  • Cons: Safari download attribute quirks cross-origin
  • Cons: Client-generated CSV must not trust unsanitized user input in filename
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • download attribute? Suggests filename for same-origin URLs; cross-origin may ignore.
  • Service Worker intercept download? Can — useful for offline cached PDFs.
Metrics to mention
  • Export job duration
  • OOM errors on large reports

Closing: For big data: server-side async job + poll + signed URL download.

19. Page unload — beforeunload, pagehide, visibilitychange
How to open (30 sec)

Unload is unreliable especially mobile — prefer visibility and sendBeacon.

Timeline — draw left-to-right while speaking (5 min)
  1. visibilitychange — Tab hidden — pause video, reduce polling frequency.
  2. pagehide — Navigating away — bfcache eligible unless blocked.
  3. beforeunload — Can show confirm dialog — heavily restricted; harms bfcache.
  4. unload — Unreliable on mobile — don't depend on critical saves here.
Deep dive (interviewer picks one)

sendBeacon: Fire-and-forget analytics on pagehide — doesn't block navigation.

Verify in DevTools
  • Console — warn if beforeunload registered
  • Test mobile Safari background tab behavior
Pitfalls & trade-offs
  • Sync XHR on unload — deprecated blocked
  • beforeunload for "unsaved changes" on every navigation
Production example — Instagram

Context: Draft post autosave debounced — no beforeunload modal; visibilitychange pauses video upload polling when tab hidden.

Why they chose this approach: sendBeacon for abandon analytics; keepalive fetch for critical save on pagehide.

  • Pros: bfcache eligible — back navigation fast
  • Pros: Upload resumes when tab visible again
  • Pros: No annoying "leave site?" on every navigation
  • Cons: Users may leave before debounced save flushes — need shorter debounce on blur
  • Cons: sendBeacon payload size limits
  • Cons: Unload unreliable on mobile — visibility API primary
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Autosave pattern? Debounced save to localStorage/API — no modal needed.
  • keepalive fetch? fetch(..., { keepalive: true }) alternative to sendBeacon with POST body limits.
Metrics to mention
  • Analytics delivery rate on bounce
  • bfcache eligibility after removing beforeunload

Closing: Replace unload hacks with Page Lifecycle API patterns.

20. Debug slow page load — full senior process
How to open (30 sec)

Structure answer as measure → hypothesize → fix → verify — not random optimizations.

Timeline — draw left-to-right while speaking (5 min)
  1. Reproduce — Incognito, throttle Fast 3G + 4x CPU, disable extensions.
  2. Network waterfall — DNS/connect/TTFB — too many origins? redirect chain? huge JS?
  3. Main thread — Performance trace — long tasks, layout thrashing, expensive selectors.
  4. CWV mapping — LCP element late? CLS from images/fonts? INP from handlers?
  5. Fix priority — Don't code-split if TTFB is 3s. Fix server/CDN first, then render path, then JS.
  6. Verify — Lighthouse CI, RUM before/after, synthetic checks in deploy pipeline.
Deep dive (interviewer picks one)

Example narrative: "LCP was hero image at 4.2s — missing preload, served JPEG not AVIF, TTFB 800ms from uncached SSR. Fixed: edge cache HTML, preload AVIF srcset, image CDN — LCP 1.8s."

Verify in DevTools
  • Network + Performance + Lighthouse together
  • WebPageTest filmstrip for stakeholder reports
  • CrUX field data vs lab
Pitfalls & trade-offs
  • Optimizing bundle before fixing 3s backend
  • Chasing green Lighthouse on fake throttling only
Production example — Netflix

Context: Slow title start — debug: TTFB on browse API vs manifest CDN vs main-thread long tasks from personalization JS.

Why they chose this approach: Measure-first: Network waterfall + Performance trace; fix dominant bucket before micro-optimizations.

  • Pros: Fixed LCP on browse by preloading hero + edge SSR
  • Pros: Playback start improved by manifest prefetch at 90% progress
  • Pros: RUM CrUX proves p75 LCP under target post-fix
  • Cons: Lab Lighthouse alone missed CDN regional regression
  • Cons: Over-indexing on bundle size while TTFB was 2s
  • Cons: Too many metrics without one owner per bottleneck
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Field vs lab? Lab reproduces; field (CrUX) shows real user percentiles — both needed.
  • Regression prevention? Bundle size budget, LCP budget in CI, RUM alerts on p75.
Metrics to mention
  • LCP p75
  • INP p75
  • TTFB p75
  • JS weight
  • Third-party long task time

Closing: Close: "I'd document bottleneck, ship smallest fix with highest CWV impact, validate in RUM within 48h."

Package Managers & Node Tooling (22) Must know

1. npm vs pnpm vs Yarn vs Bun — compare for production monorepos.
Why this question matters Package manager choice affects CI speed, disk usage, phantom dependency bugs, and supply-chain reproducibility. Senior engineers defend a choice with metrics, not preference.
ToolInstall modelStrengthsWatch-outs
npmFlat node_modules (v7+)Default, universal, npm ciSlower CI, duplicate deps, phantom deps possible
pnpmContent-addressable store + symlinksFast, strict, disk efficientSome tools assume flat node_modules
Yarn Berry (v3+)Plug'n'Play or node_modulesConstraints, workspacesPnP compatibility, learning curve
BunFast installer + runtimeSpeed benchmarksEcosystem maturity for enterprise
Example decision narrative

"We migrated 3 apps to pnpm workspaces: install time 4m→90s in CI, disk 40% smaller. We set shamefully-hoist=false default strict — fixed 12 phantom dependency bugs. Kept npm for legacy publish script until verified."

Trade-off pnpm strictness breaks packages that import undeclared deps — fix upstream or use pnpm.overrides / public-hoist-pattern for offenders like older React Native libs.
Production example — Amazon

Context: Monorepo moved to pnpm — strict node_modules caught phantom lodash imports that npm hoisting hid.

Why they chose this approach: pnpm + frozen-lockfile in CI across 50+ internal packages.

  • Pros: Faster CI install from content-addressable store
  • Pros: Phantom deps fail at install not in prod
  • Cons: Peer dep strictness breaks poorly declared libs
  • Cons: Team retraining from npm habits
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. What are phantom dependencies and how does pnpm prevent them?
Phantom dependency: Your code imports lodash but it's not in your package.json — it "leaks" from a dependency's nested node_modules because npm hoists everything flat. Works locally until dependency stops shipping lodash → prod breaks.
Example failure 
// your-app/src/util.ts
import debounce from 'lodash/debounce'; // NOT in your package.json
// npm: works if any dependency hoists lodash
// pnpm: MODULE_NOT_FOUND — forces explicit dependency
# Fix — declare explicitly
pnpm add lodash
# Or intentional hoist for legacy (last resort)
# .npmrc
public-hoist-pattern[]=*eslint*

Interview answer: "Strict node_modules catches missing declarations early — that's a feature."

Production example — Walmart

Context: Legacy app imported lodash never declared in package.json — passed locally, failed in CI strict mode.

Why they chose this approach: pnpm isolated node_modules exposes undeclared imports at install.

  • Pros: Supply-chain audit knows real dependency graph
  • Pros: CI catches missing deps before deploy
  • Cons: Migration pain fixing hundreds of phantom imports
  • Cons: Some CJS packages break without shameful hoisting
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. lockfiles — package-lock.json vs pnpm-lock.yaml vs yarn.lock.
Purpose: Reproducible installs — exact resolved versions across machines and CI. Never gitignore lockfiles in applications.
CI commands 
npm ci          # deletes node_modules, installs exact from lock — faster than install
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile  # fails if lock out of sync with package.json
yarn install --immutable
Merge conflicts Regenerate lock after resolving package.json — don't hand-edit lock. In monorepos, one lock at root (pnpm/yarn) vs npm per package (avoid).
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Big Billion Days deploy requires frozen-lockfile in CI — any drift blocks release.

Why they chose this approach: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile gate before webpack/vite build.

  • Pros: Reproducible builds across 200+ engineers
  • Pros: No "works on my machine" during sale prep
  • Cons: Lockfile merge conflicts spike before sale
  • Cons: Emergency hotfix needs deliberate lockfile update
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. npm workspaces vs pnpm workspaces — monorepo setup.
pnpm workspace (common 2025 pattern) 
// pnpm-workspace.yaml
packages:
  - 'apps/*'
  - 'packages/*'

// root package.json
{ "scripts": { "build": "pnpm -r run build" } }

// apps/web/package.json
{ "dependencies": { "@org/ui": "workspace:*" } }
Why pnpm for monorepos: Single store dedupes identical versions across packages; symlinks are instant; pnpm --filter web dev runs one app. Turborepo/Nx orchestrate tasks on top.
Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion web + seller portal + design-system packages in one pnpm workspace with catalog: protocol.

Why they chose this approach: Shared React version pinned in pnpm catalog — one bump updates all apps.

  • Pros: Single PR upgrades React across consumer + seller
  • Pros: Local workspace:* links instant DS iteration
  • Cons: Broken catalog entry blocks all apps
  • Cons: Turborepo cache invalidation complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. peerDependencies — why React libs use them.
Library shouldn't bundle its own React — two Reacts = "Invalid hook call". Peer dep says "host app provides react@^18 || ^19".
// packages/ui/package.json
"peerDependencies": { "react": "^18 || ^19", "react-dom": "^18 || ^19" },
"devDependencies": { "react": "^19.0.0" } // for local Storybook only

pnpm: peers must be resolvable from consumer — use auto-install-peers=true in .npmrc or document install steps.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Web app enforces react/react-dom peers via pnpm peerDependencyRules — one React copy for feed.

Why they chose this approach: Strict peers prevent duplicate React hook crashes in federated widgets.

  • Pros: Hooks work across feed + Reels micro-bundles
  • Pros: Clear error when peer range mismatches
  • Cons: Peer warnings block install in strict CI
  • Cons: Legacy libs with loose peers need overrides
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
6. overrides / resolutions — force transitive dependency version.
Security patch in nested dep before upstream fixes — force safe version org-wide.
// npm package.json
"overrides": { "lodash": "4.17.21", "webpack": { "acorn": "8.14.0" } }
// pnpm
"pnpm": { "overrides": { "lodash@*": "4.17.21" } }
// yarn
"resolutions": { "lodash": "4.17.21" }
Risk: Overrides can break semver contracts — run full test suite + note in ADR why override exists.
Production example — Netflix

Context: Player SDK semver — caret on internal packages with automated Renovate PRs and e2e smoke.

Why they chose this approach: Caret for 1.x libs; exact pin for compliance-critical payment SDKs.

  • Pros: Security patches flow via minor bumps
  • Pros: Renovate groups reduce PR noise
  • Cons: Caret on 0.x can pull breaking changes
  • Cons: Too many Renovate PRs fatigue teams
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
7. npm ci vs npm install in CI pipelines.
npm ci requires package-lock.json, never modifies it, deletes existing node_modules first — deterministic and faster. npm install may update lock — only for local dev when adding deps.
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
- uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
- run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
- run: pnpm --filter web test
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Big Billion Days deploy requires frozen-lockfile in CI — any drift blocks release.

Why they chose this approach: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile gate before webpack/vite build.

  • Pros: Reproducible builds across 200+ engineers
  • Pros: No "works on my machine" during sale prep
  • Cons: Lockfile merge conflicts spike before sale
  • Cons: Emergency hotfix needs deliberate lockfile update
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
8. npx vs pnpm dlx vs yarn dlx — run packages without global install.
npx create-vite@latest my-app      # npm — may cache old npx
pnpm dlx create-vite my-app        # downloads, runs, no global pollution
pnpm exec vitest run               # run local bin from node_modules/.bin
Always pin version in CI scripts: pnpm dlx create-vite@5.4.11 — avoids surprise breaking changes.
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: npx vs pnpm dlx vs yarn dlx — run packages without global install..

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
9. .npmrc / .pnpmfile.cjs — common production settings.
# .npmrc
engine-strict=true
save-exact=true           # npm — pin exact versions (team policy)
auto-install-peers=true   # pnpm
strict-peer-dependencies=false
registry=https://registry.npmjs.org/
//registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=${NPM_TOKEN}
engine-strict fails install if Node version wrong — matches "engines": { "node": ">=20" } in package.json.
Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: .npmrc / .pnpmfile.cjs — common production settings..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
10. Supply chain — npm audit, Socket, lockfile integrity.
Senior FE owns dependency risk — not just backend. CI should fail on critical CVEs in direct/transitive deps (with override path documented).
pnpm audit --audit-level=high
# package.json allowlist only with ticket reference
# Use Renovate grouped PRs — not Dependabot spam 50/week
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Big Billion Days deploy requires frozen-lockfile in CI — any drift blocks release.

Why they chose this approach: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile gate before webpack/vite build.

  • Pros: Reproducible builds across 200+ engineers
  • Pros: No "works on my machine" during sale prep
  • Cons: Lockfile merge conflicts spike before sale
  • Cons: Emergency hotfix needs deliberate lockfile update
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
11. semver caret vs tilde
Reasoning Caret ^1.2.3 allows minor/patch up to <2.0.0. Tilde ~1.2.3 allows patch only. Caret default npm — balances features and safety.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Caret ^1.2.3 allows minor/patch up to <2.0.0.
  2. Tilde ~1.2.3 allows patch only.
  3. Caret default npm — balances features and safety.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
"lodash": "^4.17.21"
Trade-off Caret can pull breaking if 0.x version (0.x behaves like major).

Pin exact for compliance-critical deps.

Production example — Netflix

Context: Player SDK semver — caret on internal packages with automated Renovate PRs and e2e smoke.

Why they chose this approach: Caret for 1.x libs; exact pin for compliance-critical payment SDKs.

  • Pros: Security patches flow via minor bumps
  • Pros: Renovate groups reduce PR noise
  • Cons: Caret on 0.x can pull breaking changes
  • Cons: Too many Renovate PRs fatigue teams
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
12. bundledDependencies
Reasoning Packages bundled inside tarball on publish — rare, increases package size.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Packages bundled inside tarball on publish — rare, increases package size.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Used for CLI shipping small deps
Trade-off Prefer normal dependencies for apps.

Know exists for npm publish questions.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: bundledDependencies.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

13. optionalDependencies
Reasoning Install continues if optional fails — platform-specific native modules (esbuild, sharp).
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Install continues if optional fails — platform-specific native modules (esbuild, sharp).

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
npm optional platform binaries
Trade-off CI must test on target OS if optional used.

Lockfile still records resolution.

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: optionalDependencies.

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.

14. postinstall script security
Reasoning Malicious package runs arbitrary code on install — supply chain vector.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Malicious package runs arbitrary code on install — supply chain vector.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
pnpm install --ignore-scripts in CI until audited
Trade-off Allowlist trusted packages scripts only.

Socket.dev / npm audit in pipeline.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Seller Central blocks postinstall scripts in CI until Socket.dev audit clears new deps.

Why they chose this approach: pnpm install --ignore-scripts in CI + allowlist native modules (esbuild, sharp).

  • Pros: Blocks malicious install-time code execution
  • Pros: Audit trail for every new dependency
  • Cons: Legitimate postinstall skipped until allowlisted
  • Cons: Slower onboarding for native modules
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
15. package.json exports field
Reasoning Controls import paths — conditional exports for import/require/browser.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Controls import paths — conditional exports for import/require/browser.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
"exports": { ".": { "import": "./dist/index.mjs", "require": "./dist/index.cjs" } }
Trade-off Breaks deep imports like pkg/dist/internal — intentional encapsulation.

Library authors must define exports correctly.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: package.json exports field.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

16. type module vs commonjs
Reasoning "type":"module" makes .js files ESM; use .cjs extension for CommonJS config files.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

"type":"module" makes .js files ESM; use .cjs extension for CommonJS config files.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
vite.config.ts ESM; jest.config.cjs CJS
Trade-off Mixed projects confusing — document in README.

Node native ESM stable — prefer ESM greenfield.

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: type module vs commonjs.

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player

17. Node ESM vs CJS interop
Reasoning import createRequire from 'module' for CJS in ESM; dynamic import() for ESM from CJS.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

import createRequire from 'module' for CJS in ESM; dynamic import() for ESM from CJS.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const require = createRequire(import.meta.url)
Trade-off Default export interop differences.

Bundler abstracts most pain for frontend.

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Node ESM vs CJS interop.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks

  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.

18. Volta vs engines field
Reasoning Volta pins node/yarn/pnpm per project in package.json — auto-switch on cd.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Volta pins node/yarn/pnpm per project in package.json — auto-switch on cd.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
"volta": { "node": "20.11.0", "pnpm": "9.0.0" }
Trade-off engines only warns unless engine-strict.

Volta better DX than nvm for teams.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Volta vs engines field.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
19. Corepack enable pnpm/yarn
Reasoning Node ships Corepack — enables packageManager field enforcement.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Node ships Corepack — enables packageManager field enforcement.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
corepack enable && corepack prepare pnpm@9 --activate
Trade-off packageManager field in package.json pins version for CI.

Node 18+ includes corepack (may need enable).

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Corepack enable pnpm/yarn.

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
20. Private registry scope
Reasoning @org packages mapped to Artifactory/GitHub Packages via .npmrc registry.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

@org packages mapped to Artifactory/GitHub Packages via .npmrc registry.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
@org:registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com
Trade-off Auth token in CI secrets — never commit.

Monorepo publishes internal DS packages.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Private registry scope.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
21. pnpm catalog (v9+)
Reasoning Centralize version definitions in pnpm-workspace.yaml — bump once update everywhere.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Centralize version definitions in pnpm-workspace.yaml — bump once update everywhere.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
catalog: { react: ^19.0.0 } then "react": "catalog:" in package.json
Trade-off Requires pnpm 9+ adoption.

Reduces version drift across monorepo.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion web + seller portal + design-system packages in one pnpm workspace with catalog: protocol.

Why they chose this approach: Shared React version pinned in pnpm catalog — one bump updates all apps.

  • Pros: Single PR upgrades React across consumer + seller
  • Pros: Local workspace:* links instant DS iteration
  • Cons: Broken catalog entry blocks all apps
  • Cons: Turborepo cache invalidation complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
22. Interview narrative npm vs pnpm
Reasoning Structure answer: problem (phantom deps, CI time) → action (migrate, frozen lockfile, cache) → result (metrics) → trade-off (fixed 3 hoisting issues).
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Structure answer: problem (phantom deps, CI time) → action (migrate, frozen lockfile, cache) → result (metrics) → trade-off (fixed 3 hoisting issues).

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Install 4m→90s, disk -40%, 12 undeclared deps caught
Trade-off Honesty about migration cost shows seniority.

This is THE package manager question format.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Monorepo moved to pnpm — strict node_modules caught phantom lodash imports that npm hoisting hid.

Why they chose this approach: pnpm + frozen-lockfile in CI across 50+ internal packages.

  • Pros: Faster CI install from content-addressable store
  • Pros: Phantom deps fail at install not in prod
  • Cons: Peer dep strictness breaks poorly declared libs
  • Cons: Team retraining from npm habits
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • CI enforcement? pnpm install --frozen-lockfile, audit gate, engines-strict in pipeline — fail build not warn.
  • If ignored? Phantom deps break prod, lockfile drift causes "works on my machine", supply-chain audit misses CVEs.
  • npm vs pnpm here? pnpm: content-addressable store, strict node_modules, faster CI — npm: wider default compatibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

Frontend Engineering Tooling (20)

1. ESLint + Prettier + TypeScript — how they fit together.
ESLint catches bugs and patterns (hooks rules, import order). Prettier formats whitespace — don't fight ESLint stylistic rules (turn off formatting in ESLint). TypeScript is static analysis — complementary, not replacement.
// eslint.config.js (flat config)
import tseslint from 'typescript-eslint';
export default tseslint.config(
  ...tseslint.configs.recommended,
  { rules: { 'react-hooks/exhaustive-deps': 'error' } }
);
// package.json
"lint": "eslint src && tsc --noEmit"
Production example — Netflix

Context: PR pipeline: ESLint a11y + bundle budget + Playwright smoke on signup + MSW contract tests.

Why they chose this approach: Golden path template repo every team forks.

  • Pros: Catch chunk 404 before prod
  • Pros: Uniform observability tags
  • Cons: Pipeline slow if not parallelized
  • Cons: Flaky e2e blocks releases
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Success metric? CI time down, flake rate down, deploy frequency up, mean time to restore — pick one quantified outcome.
  • Rollback plan? Feature flag off, revert deploy, pin previous Docker image — never "fix forward" without mitigation path.
  • Documentation? ADRs, README in repo root, onboarding checklist, runbook linked from PR template.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. Husky + lint-staged — pre-commit without slowing devs.
// lint-staged.config.js
export default {
  '*.{ts,tsx}': ['eslint --fix', 'prettier --write'],
  '*.{ts,tsx}': () => 'tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit' // once per commit
};
// .husky/pre-commit
pnpm lint-staged
Heavy tsc on every file change is slow — run full typecheck in CI; lint-staged only affected files locally.
Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Husky + lint-staged — pre-commit without slowing devs..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Success metric? CI time down, flake rate down, deploy frequency up, mean time to restore — pick one quantified outcome.
  • Rollback plan? Feature flag off, revert deploy, pin previous Docker image — never "fix forward" without mitigation path.
  • Documentation? ADRs, README in repo root, onboarding checklist, runbook linked from PR template.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. Changesets vs semantic-release for versioning monorepos.
Changesets: Human writes changeset file describing bump per package — good for multi-package with mixed release cadence.semantic-release: Fully automated from conventional commits — single app repos.
Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Changesets vs semantic-release for versioning monorepos..

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Success metric? CI time down, flake rate down, deploy frequency up, mean time to restore — pick one quantified outcome.
  • Rollback plan? Feature flag off, revert deploy, pin previous Docker image — never "fix forward" without mitigation path.
  • Documentation? ADRs, README in repo root, onboarding checklist, runbook linked from PR template.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. Turborepo vs Nx — task orchestration.
// turbo.json
{ "pipeline": { "build": { "dependsOn": ["^build"], "outputs": ["dist/**"] }, "test": { "dependsOn": ["build"] } } }
Remote cache (Vercel) shares build artifacts across CI runs — 10min build → 2min cache hit. Nx adds module boundary enforcement + generators — pick based on team scale.
Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Turborepo vs Nx — task orchestration..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Success metric? CI time down, flake rate down, deploy frequency up, mean time to restore — pick one quantified outcome.
  • Rollback plan? Feature flag off, revert deploy, pin previous Docker image — never "fix forward" without mitigation path.
  • Documentation? ADRs, README in repo root, onboarding checklist, runbook linked from PR template.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. Environment variables — Vite vs Next vs Webpack.
# Vite — only VITE_ exposed to client
VITE_API_URL=https://api.example.com
// import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL

# Next — NEXT_PUBLIC_ for browser
NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL=...

# Never expose secrets — server-only vars without prefix

6. Git merge vs rebase
Reasoning Rebase linear history feature branch; never rebase shared main.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Rebase linear history feature branch; never rebase shared main.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
git fetch origin main && git rebase origin/main

Conflict resolution skill expected senior.

  • Pros: Uniform observability tags
  • Cons: Pipeline slow if not parallelized
  • Cons: Flaky e2e blocks releases
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Success metric? CI time down, flake rate down, deploy frequency up, mean time to restore — pick one quantified outcome.
  • Rollback plan? Feature flag off, revert deploy, pin previous Docker image — never "fix forward" without mitigation path.
  • Documentation? ADRs, README in repo root, onboarding checklist, runbook linked from PR template.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

React (55 questions)

Core & Reconciliation

1. Explain the React reconciliation algorithm and what triggers a re-render.
Reasoning React maintains a Fiber tree — each node is a unit of work with links to child, sibling, and return (parent). On state update, React schedules a render pass that calls your component functions to produce a new element tree, then diffs against the previous tree. Only changed DOM nodes are patched in the commit phase. Re-render means your function ran again; DOM update may not happen if output is identical.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. React maintains a Fiber tree — each node is a unit of work with links to child, sibling, and return (parent).
  2. On state update, React schedules a render pass that calls your component functions to produce a new element tree, then diffs against the previous tree.
  3. Only changed DOM nodes are patched in the commit phase.
  4. Re-render means your function ran again; DOM update may not happen if output is identical.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example 
function Parent() {
  const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
  return (
    <>
      <button onClick={() => setCount(c => c + 1)}>+</button>
      <Child count={count} />  {/* re-renders every Parent click */}
      <Child count={0} />     {/* also re-renders — same props ref? still re-renders if Parent re-rendered */}
    </>
  );
}
const Child = memo(function Child({ count }) {
  console.log('Child render', count);
  return <span>{count}</span>;
});

Triggers: useState/useReducer dispatch, context value change, parent re-render, forceUpdate, class setState.

Trade-off Manual memo everywhere adds comparison cost and bugs (unstable inline objects). React 19 Compiler can auto-memo — still verify with Profiler on hot paths.

Staff-level close: mention render phase (pure) vs commit phase (DOM mutations, refs, layout effects).

Production example — Instagram

Context: Feed list re-rendered on every like — memoized PostRow + selector from external store cut commits 40%.

Why they chose this approach: React.memo on row + useSyncExternalStore for like counts.

  • Pros: Scroll stays 60fps on mid-tier phones
  • Pros: Profiler proved Table 40ms → 8ms
  • Cons: Premature memo everywhere hurts readability
  • Cons: Stale memo if props compare shallow wrong
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. What changed in React 19 that affects production apps?
Reasoning React 19 focuses on async UX, less boilerplate, and compiler-driven optimization. Production impact: forms/actions, optimistic patterns built-in, ref simplification, and stricter dev StrictMode for effect cleanup.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. React 19 focuses on async UX, less boilerplate, and compiler-driven optimization.
  2. Production impact: forms/actions, optimistic patterns built-in, ref simplification, and stricter dev StrictMode for effect cleanup.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

  • use() — read promises/context conditionally in render (Suspense boundary required for promises)
  • useOptimistic / useActionState — pending states without manual flags
  • Ref as normal prop — drop forwardRef on leaf components
  • React Compiler (optional) — compile-time memoization
Example — optimistic cart update 
const [optimisticItems, addOptimistic] = useOptimistic(items, (state, newItem) => [...state, newItem]);

async function addToCart(formData) {
  addOptimistic({ id: 'temp', name: formData.get('name') });
  await createCartItem(formData); // revert handled by refresh or error boundary
}
Migration risk Third-party libs not yet compatible with React 19 concurrent features — test map/chart libs under StrictMode before upgrading.
Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: What changed in React 19 that affects production apps?.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. Controlled vs uncontrolled components — when to use each?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Controlled: React owns value via state — predictable, testable, validation on change.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Controlled: React owns value via state — predictable, testable, validation on change.

const [email, setEmail] = useState('');
<input value={email} onChange={e => setEmail(e.target.value)} />

Uncontrolled: DOM owns value — refs for file inputs, third-party widgets, perf on huge forms.

const ref = useRef(null);
<input defaultValue="a@b.com" ref={ref} />
// submit: ref.current.value
Production example — Myntra

Context: Size selector on PDP — controlled for validation; file upload review photos uncontrolled ref.

Why they chose this approach: React Hook Form on 30-field seller onboarding — fewer re-renders.

  • Pros: INP good on long forms
  • Pros: Zod resolver at boundary
  • Cons: Uncontrolled harder to unit test pure
  • Cons: Third-party widgets need refs
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. Explain useEffect vs useLayoutEffect vs useInsertionEffect.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

useEffect: after paint, async — data fetch, subscriptions, non-visual side effects.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

useEffect: after paint, async — data fetch, subscriptions, non-visual side effects.

useLayoutEffect: after DOM update, before paint — measure DOM, sync scroll, prevent flicker.

useInsertionEffect: before layout, for CSS-in-JS injecting styles.

useLayoutEffect(() => {
  const h = ref.current.getBoundingClientRect().height;
  setHeight(h); // user won't see wrong layout flash
}, []);
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Explain useEffect vs useLayoutEffect vs useInsertionEffect..

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. Why does React 18 StrictMode double-invoke effects in development?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. To surface non-idempotent effects (missing cleanup, duplicate subscriptions, stale closures).
  2. Production runs once.
  3. Map libraries and WebSockets must clean up properly.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

To surface non-idempotent effects (missing cleanup, duplicate subscriptions, stale closures). Production runs once. Map libraries and WebSockets must clean up properly.

useEffect(() => {
  const ws = new WebSocket(url);
  return () => ws.close(); // REQUIRED
}, [url]);
Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Why does React 18 StrictMode double-invoke effects in development?.

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
6. What is the React Fiber architecture?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Fiber is a unit of work — linked list of nodes enabling incremental rendering, priority scheduling, pause/resume, and concurrent features.
  2. Each fiber has alternate (double buffering), child/sibling/return pointers.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Fiber is a unit of work — linked list of nodes enabling incremental rendering, priority scheduling, pause/resume, and concurrent features. Each fiber has alternate (double buffering), child/sibling/return pointers.

Interview depth: mention lanes (priority), work loop (sync vs concurrent), commit phase (mutation + layout + passive effects).

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: What is the React Fiber architecture?.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
7. Explain Concurrent React and time slicing.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. React can interrupt low-priority renders for urgent updates (input).
  2. startTransition marks non-urgent state updates.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

React can interrupt low-priority renders for urgent updates (input). startTransition marks non-urgent state updates.

const [query, setQuery] = useState('');
const [results, setResults] = useState([]);
const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition();

const onChange = (v) => {
  setQuery(v); // urgent — input stays responsive
  startTransition(() => setResults(filterHugeList(v))); // interruptible
};
Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Explain Concurrent React and time slicing..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
8. useMemo vs useCallback vs React.memo — when each matters.
const sorted = useMemo(() => items.sort(cmp), [items]); // expensive compute
const onSave = useCallback(() => save(id), [id]);       // stable ref for deps/memo child
const Row = memo(function Row({ item }) { return <tr>...</tr>; }); // skip re-render

Don't blanket-memo everything — measure first. Compiler may make manual memo redundant.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Feed list re-rendered on every like — memoized PostRow + selector from external store cut commits 40%.

Why they chose this approach: React.memo on row + useSyncExternalStore for like counts.

  • Pros: Scroll stays 60fps on mid-tier phones
  • Pros: Profiler proved Table 40ms → 8ms
  • Cons: Premature memo everywhere hurts readability
  • Cons: Stale memo if props compare shallow wrong
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
9. How do you prevent unnecessary re-renders in a large context tree?
  • Split contexts (state vs dispatch)
  • useContextSelector pattern or Zustand/Jotai
  • Memoize provider value carefully
  • Colocate state
// Split pattern
const StateCtx = createContext(null);
const DispatchCtx = createContext(null);
// consumers of dispatch don't re-render on state change
Production example — Instagram

Context: Feed list re-rendered on every like — memoized PostRow + selector from external store cut commits 40%.

Why they chose this approach: React.memo on row + useSyncExternalStore for like counts.

  • Pros: Scroll stays 60fps on mid-tier phones
  • Pros: Profiler proved Table 40ms → 8ms
  • Cons: Premature memo everywhere hurts readability
  • Cons: Stale memo if props compare shallow wrong
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
10. Explain keys in lists — why not use index?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Keys identify identity across renders.
  2. Index keys break on insert/reorder — wrong component state preserved, poor perf.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Keys identify identity across renders. Index keys break on insert/reorder — wrong component state preserved, poor perf.

// BAD: reorder causes wrong row selection state
items.map((item, i) => <Row key={i} item={item} />)
// GOOD:
items.map(item => <Row key={item.id} item={item} />)
Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Explain keys in lists — why not use index?.

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

Hooks Deep Dive

11. Rules of Hooks — why do they exist?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Hooks stored in linked list per component instance.
  2. Order must be stable every render — no hooks in loops/conditions/nested functions.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Hooks stored in linked list per component instance. Order must be stable every render — no hooks in loops/conditions/nested functions.

// WRONG
if (loggedIn) useEffect(() => {}, []);
// RIGHT
useEffect(() => { if (loggedIn) subscribe(); return cleanup; }, [loggedIn]);
Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Rules of Hooks — why do they exist?.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
12. Custom hook design — what makes a good custom hook?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Encapsulates stateful logic, not UI.
  2. Returns stable API.
  3. Handles cleanup.
  4. Name starts with use .

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Encapsulates stateful logic, not UI. Returns stable API. Handles cleanup. Name starts with use.

function useDebouncedValue<T>(value: T, ms = 300) {
  const [debounced, setDebounced] = useState(value);
  useEffect(() => {
    const t = setTimeout(() => setDebounced(value), ms);
    return () => clearTimeout(t);
  }, [value, ms]);
  return debounced;
}
Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Custom hook design — what makes a good custom hook?.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
13. useRef vs useState — storage and re-render implications.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. useRef mutates .current without re-render — timers, previous values, DOM nodes.
  2. useState triggers re-render on update.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

useRef mutates .current without re-render — timers, previous values, DOM nodes. useState triggers re-render on update.

const renderCount = useRef(0);
renderCount.current += 1; // no re-render from this
Production example — Instagram

Context: Feed list re-rendered on every like — memoized PostRow + selector from external store cut commits 40%.

Why they chose this approach: React.memo on row + useSyncExternalStore for like counts.

  • Pros: Scroll stays 60fps on mid-tier phones
  • Pros: Profiler proved Table 40ms → 8ms
  • Cons: Premature memo everywhere hurts readability
  • Cons: Stale memo if props compare shallow wrong
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
14. useReducer vs useState for complex state.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

useReducer when: next state depends on previous, multiple sub-values, complex transitions, testable pure reducer.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

useReducer when: next state depends on previous, multiple sub-values, complex transitions, testable pure reducer.

function reducer(state, action) {
  switch (action.type) {
    case 'increment': return { ...state, count: state.count + 1 };
    default: return state;
  }
}
const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, { count: 0 });
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: useReducer vs useState for complex state..

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React 19 Compiler? If Compiler auto-memoizes, redundant useMemo/useCallback adds noise — Profile before/after and delete manual memo where Compiler proves stable.
  • Profiler proof? Record interaction, sort by render duration, cite component name and ms saved after fix (e.g. context split or selector).
  • Breaks at scale? Usually context too high, list without virtualization, or derived state in render — name the scaling trigger explicitly.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
15. How does useId work and why use it over Math.random()?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Stable unique IDs across SSR/client hydration.
  2. Avoids mismatch.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Stable unique IDs across SSR/client hydration. Avoids mismatch.

const id = useId();
<label htmlFor={id}>Email</label>
<input id={id} type="email" />
Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: How does useId work and why use it over Math.random()?.

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
