  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
9. Redux DevTools — time travel and action replay.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Records action stream; jump to state; import/export for bug reports.
  2. configureStore enables by default in dev.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Records action stream; jump to state; import/export for bug reports. configureStore enables by default in dev.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Redux DevTools — time travel and action replay..

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
10. Immer in createSlice — how draft works.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Write "mutating" code on Proxy draft; Immer produces immutable next state.
  2. Can't mutate non-draft returns; async needs separate thunk.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Write "mutating" code on Proxy draft; Immer produces immutable next state. Can't mutate non-draft returns; async needs separate thunk.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Immer in createSlice — how draft works..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
11. Action creators in Redux
Reasoning Functions returning plain { type, payload } objects. RTK createSlice auto-generates creators from reducers.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Functions returning plain { type, payload } objects.
  2. RTK createSlice auto-generates creators from reducers.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
dispatch(increment()) // vs dispatch({ type: 'counter/increment' })
Trade-off Over-abstraction if one-liner — RTK reduces need.

Keep action names domain-scoped: orders/assign.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Action creators in Redux.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
12. combineReducers composition
Reasoning Maps keys to slice reducers — each slice independent. Root state typed as combined shape.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Maps keys to slice reducers — each slice independent.
  2. Root state typed as combined shape.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
combineReducers({ auth: authReducer, cart: cartReducer })
Trade-off Cross-slice logic belongs in middleware/listeners not reducer imports.

RTK configureStore accepts reducer object directly.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: combineReducers composition.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
13. redux-persist
Reasoning Persists slice to localStorage/IndexedDB, rehydrates on load. Whitelist slices — don't persist sensitive tokens raw.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Persists slice to localStorage/IndexedDB, rehydrates on load.
  2. Whitelist slices — don't persist sensitive tokens raw.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
persistReducer({ key: 'root', storage, whitelist: ['cart'] }, rootReducer)
Trade-off Stale persisted schema — version migrations needed.

Encrypt persist for PII if required.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Wishlist redux-persist whitelist — not auth tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Persist cart UI prefs only; tokens HttpOnly cookie.

  • Pros: Wishlist survives refresh
  • Pros: No XSS token theft from localStorage
  • Cons: Schema migration on persist version
  • Cons: Stale persisted state after deploy
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
14. SSR Redux hydration
Reasoning Server renders with preloadedState from request; client createStore(preloadedState) must match HTML.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Server renders with preloadedState from request; client createStore(preloadedState) must match HTML.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const store = configureStore({ preloadedState: window.__PRELOADED_STATE__ });
Trade-off Mismatch causes duplicate fetch or flash.

Strip secrets from serialized state.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: SSR Redux hydration.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
15. Selector colocation
Reasoning selectors live in slice file with createSelector — co-locate with state shape they read.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

selectors live in slice file with createSelector — co-locate with state shape they read.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
export const selectCartTotal = createSelector(selectItems, items =&gt; sum(items))
Trade-off Circular imports if slices cross-reference — use entity adapter patterns.

Test selectors as pure functions.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Selector colocation.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
16. Ducks / feature folder pattern
Reasoning Slice + selectors + thunks + components in features/orders/ — scalable team ownership.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Slice + selectors + thunks + components in features/orders/ — scalable team ownership.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
features/orders/ordersSlice.ts, OrdersList.tsx
Trade-off Deep nesting without boundaries — enforce public API index.ts.

Nx module boundaries enforce imports.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Ducks / feature folder pattern.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
17. Giant slice anti-pattern
Reasoning Single slice for entire app — hard to maintain, broad re-renders if poorly selected.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Single slice for entire app — hard to maintain, broad re-renders if poorly selected.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Split: authSlice, ordersSlice, uiSlice
Trade-off Too many slices — overhead negligible vs god slice.

Match slice to domain bounded context.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Giant slice anti-pattern.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
18. Side effects NEVER in reducer
Reasoning Reducers must be pure sync functions — no fetch, no Date.now() hidden side effects.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Reducers must be pure sync functions — no fetch, no Date.now() hidden side effects.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Use createAsyncThunk or listener middleware instead
Trade-off Impure reducer breaks time-travel debugging.

Interview red flag if candidate puts API in reducer.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Listener middleware fires analytics toast on rejected payment thunk — central error UX.

Why they chose this approach: Side effects in listener, never in reducer.

  • Pros: Pure reducers testable
  • Pros: Cross-cutting analytics one place
  • Cons: Listener spaghetti if overused
  • Cons: Harder to trace than colocated useEffect
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
19. RTK listener middleware
Reasoning Declarative when(action, effect) — replaces many custom middlewares.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Declarative when(action, effect) — replaces many custom middlewares.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
listenerMiddleware.startListening({ matcher: isAnyOf(fetched), effect: async (a, api) =&gt; {} })
Trade-off Over-listening causes effect spaghetti — document triggers.

Great for cross-cutting: analytics, toasts.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Listener middleware fires analytics toast on rejected payment thunk — central error UX.

Why they chose this approach: Side effects in listener, never in reducer.

  • Pros: Pure reducers testable
  • Pros: Cross-cutting analytics one place
  • Cons: Listener spaghetti if overused
  • Cons: Harder to trace than colocated useEffect
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
20. RTK Query optimistic updates
Reasoning onQueryStarted patches cache optimistically, undo on error with patchResult.undo()
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

onQueryStarted patches cache optimistically, undo on error with patchResult.undo()

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
api.util.updateQueryData('getPost', id, draft =&gt; { draft.title = 'new' })
Trade-off Complex rollback — prefer pessimistic for payments.

Tags + invalidation simpler for many cases.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Seller inventory RTK Query tags — mutation invalidates ["products", sku] list and detail.

Why they chose this approach: Tags + invalidates over manual refetch in components.

  • Pros: Consistent cache after stock update
  • Pros: Optimistic qty with rollback on 409
  • Cons: Tag graph complex at scale
  • Cons: Over-invalidation causes fetch storms
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
21. Redux vs Context
Reasoning Redux: middleware, devtools, selectors, predictable updates at scale. Context: simpler injection, worse for frequent updates without split.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Redux: middleware, devtools, selectors, predictable updates at scale.
  2. Context: simpler injection, worse for frequent updates without split.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
Cart with 50 updates/min → external store or Query not mega Context
Trade-off Redux boilerplate reduced by RTK.

2025: Query for server, Zustand for UI, Redux if already invested.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Redux vs Context.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
22. Redux vs Zustand
Reasoning Zustand: minimal API, hooks, no Provider required, good for UI state.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Zustand: minimal API, hooks, no Provider required, good for UI state.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const useStore = create(set =&gt; ({ count: 0, inc: () =&gt; set(s =&gt; ({ count: s.count+1 })) }))
Trade-off Less ecosystem for time-travel enterprise tooling.

Pick Zustand for new small-medium apps.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Redux vs Zustand.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
23. Redux vs Jotai/Recoil
Reasoning Atomic state — component subscribes to atoms, fine-grained updates without selectors.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Atomic state — component subscribes to atoms, fine-grained updates without selectors.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
atom family per entity ID — good for spreadsheet cells
Trade-off Different mental model from flux.

Know concept even if team uses Redux.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Redux vs Jotai/Recoil.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity

  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
24. Batching in React 18 + Redux
Reasoning Multiple dispatches in async handler batch to one React render automatically.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Multiple dispatches in async handler batch to one React render automatically.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
dispatch(a()); dispatch(b()); // one render
Trade-off React-Redux v8+ uses sync external store correctly.

Legacy unstable_batchedUpdates rarely needed.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Batching in React 18 + Redux.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.

25. Serializable check middleware
Reasoning RTK warns if action/state contains Date, Map, function, Promise — breaks persistence/devtools.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

RTK warns if action/state contains Date, Map, function, Promise — breaks persistence/devtools.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
store serializableCheck: { ignoredPaths: ['meta.timestamp'] }
Trade-off Ignore paths sparingly with comment why.

Convert Dates to ISO strings in actions.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Listener middleware fires analytics toast on rejected payment thunk — central error UX.

Why they chose this approach: Side effects in listener, never in reducer.

  • Pros: Pure reducers testable
  • Pros: Cross-cutting analytics one place
  • Cons: Listener spaghetti if overused
  • Cons: Harder to trace than colocated useEffect
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.

26. Auth token in RTK Query baseQuery
Reasoning prepareHeaders injects Authorization from state or cookie-aware fetch.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

prepareHeaders injects Authorization from state or cookie-aware fetch.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({ prepareHeaders: (h, { getState }) =&gt; { h.set('Authorization', token(getState())); } })
Trade-off Token in Redux state — XSS can steal; prefer HttpOnly cookie + BFF.

401 handler dispatches logout via listener.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Seller inventory RTK Query tags — mutation invalidates ["products", sku] list and detail.

Why they chose this approach: Tags + invalidates over manual refetch in components.

  • Pros: Consistent cache after stock update
  • Pros: Optimistic qty with rollback on 409
  • Cons: Tag graph complex at scale
  • Cons: Over-invalidation causes fetch storms
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
27. createEntityAdapter
Reasoning Normalized CRUD helpers: addOne, updateOne, removeOne, selectors for all IDs.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Normalized CRUD helpers: addOne, updateOne, removeOne, selectors for all IDs.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const adapter = createEntityAdapter&lt;User&gt;(); adapter.reducers in slice
Trade-off Overkill for 5-item list.

Standard for large normalized tables.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: createEntityAdapter.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
28. RTK Query prefetch
Reasoning Warm cache before navigation — instant page.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Warm cache before navigation — instant page.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
dispatch(api.util.prefetch('getUser', userId, { force: true }))
Trade-off Over-prefetch wastes bandwidth.

Prefetch on link hover/focus.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Seller inventory RTK Query tags — mutation invalidates ["products", sku] list and detail.

Why they chose this approach: Tags + invalidates over manual refetch in components.

  • Pros: Consistent cache after stock update
  • Pros: Optimistic qty with rollback on 409
  • Cons: Tag graph complex at scale
  • Cons: Over-invalidation causes fetch storms
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
29. injectReducer (legacy code split)
Reasoning Dynamically add reducer to store on route load — replaced by RTK Query + static slices mostly.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Dynamically add reducer to store on route load — replaced by RTK Query + static slices mostly.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
store.injectReducer('admin', adminReducer)
Trade-off TypeScript root state typing harder with injection.

Prefer route-level lazy components not lazy reducers.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: injectReducer (legacy code split).

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
30. Testing reducers
Reasoning Pure function test: given state + action → expect new state. No store needed.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Pure function test: given state + action → expect new state.
  2. No store needed.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
expect(reducer({ count: 0 }, increment())).toEqual({ count: 1 })
Trade-off Integration tests still needed for middleware.

Table-driven tests for all action types.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Testing reducers.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
31. Testing thunks
Reasoning Use real store + waitFor or mock fetch with MSW — avoid redux-mock-store for integration.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Use real store + waitFor or mock fetch with MSW — avoid redux-mock-store for integration.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
render with Provider; userEvent click; await waitFor(() =&gt; expect(screen.getByText(...)))
Trade-off Mock store doesn't run middleware realistically.

MSW + RTL preferred pattern.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Testing thunks.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
32. extraReducers cross-slice
Reasoning Slice listens to other slice actions via builder.addCase(otherAction, ...)
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Slice listens to other slice actions via builder.addCase(otherAction, ...)

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
extraReducers: (builder) =&gt; { builder.addCase(auth.logout, () =&gt; initialState); }
Trade-off Couples slices — prefer listener for loose coupling sometimes.

Clear cart on logout common pattern.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: extraReducers cross-slice.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
33. Global error slice + listener
Reasoning Central API error handler dispatches setError or toast on rejected thunks.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Central API error handler dispatches setError or toast on rejected thunks.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
listenerMiddleware.startListening({ predicate: isRejected, effect: (a) =&gt; toast.error(a.payload) })
Trade-off Generic errors lose context — include requestId.

401 triggers auth refresh flow once.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Listener middleware fires analytics toast on rejected payment thunk — central error UX.

Why they chose this approach: Side effects in listener, never in reducer.

  • Pros: Pure reducers testable
  • Pros: Cross-cutting analytics one place
  • Cons: Listener spaghetti if overused
  • Cons: Harder to trace than colocated useEffect
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
34. Undo/redo with redux-undo
Reasoning Wrap reducer with undoable() — past/future stacks.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Wrap reducer with undoable() — past/future stacks.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
import undoable from 'redux-undo'; undoable(todosReducer)
Trade-off Memory cap needed for large states.

Productivity apps only — not typical CRUD apps.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Undo/redo with redux-undo.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
35. Industry trend 2025 — Redux role
Reasoning Many teams: TanStack Query (server cache) + Zustand/Context (UI). Redux remains in large existing codebases and RTK Query shops.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Many teams: TanStack Query (server cache) + Zustand/Context (UI).
  2. Redux remains in large existing codebases and RTK Query shops.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
Greenfield: evaluate Query first; Redux if need listener middleware at scale
Trade-off Migration costly — strangler not big-bang.

Senior: know when NOT to add Redux.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Industry trend 2025 — Redux role.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield Redux? Only if team needs middleware/devtools/time-travel at scale; otherwise RTK Query + Zustand/Jotai for UI is common 2025 default.
  • RTK Query angle? Server cache replaces most hand-rolled thunks — mention tags, invalidation, and optimistic updates where relevant.
  • Testing split? Reducers/thunks unit pure; integration with MSW + RTL for user flows; avoid redux-mock-store for middleware paths.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

Webpack (30 questions)

1. Webpack core concepts: entry, output, loaders, plugins.
module.exports = {
  entry: './src/index.js',
  output: { path: path.resolve('dist'), filename: '[name].[contenthash].js', clean: true },
  module: { rules: [{ test: /\.tsx?$/, use: 'ts-loader', exclude: /node_modules/ }] },
  plugins: [new HtmlWebpackPlugin({ template: 'index.html' })]
};
Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Webpack core concepts: entry, output, loaders, plugins..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. Module federation — micro-frontends.
// Host webpack.config
new ModuleFederationPlugin({
  remotes: { shop: 'shop@https://shop.example/remoteEntry.js' },
  shared: { react: { singleton: true, requiredVersion: '^18' } }
});
// Host component: const Product = lazy(() => import('shop/Product'));
Production example — Walmart

Context: Checkout remote loaded by shell via Module Federation — shared react singleton.

Why they chose this approach: Shell owns router + auth; checkout team ships remote.

  • Pros: Independent deploy checkout
  • Pros: Shared design system version pinned in CI
  • Cons: Remote down → white screen without error boundary
  • Cons: React duplicate breaks hooks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. Contenthash vs chunkhash vs hash.
  • hash: build-wide — bad for caching
  • chunkhash: per chunk
  • contenthash: per file content — best long-term cache
Production example — Amazon

Context: Seller Central webpack splitChunks vendor + route bundles — admin analytics separate chunk.

Why they chose this approach: Route-level dynamic import in legacy Webpack app.

  • Pros: Initial login bundle small
  • Pros: Cache vendor chunk long-term
  • Cons: Chunk 404 if wrong publicPath on deploy
  • Cons: Many chunks on HTTP/1.1 — mitigated by HTTP/2
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. Tree shaking — requirements.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

ES modules, sideEffects: false in package.json, production mode, avoid barrel re-export anti-patterns, Terser dead code elimination.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

ES modules, sideEffects: false in package.json, production mode, avoid barrel re-export anti-patterns, Terser dead code elimination.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Tree shaking — requirements..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. Code splitting — dynamic import syntax.
import(/* webpackChunkName: "admin" */ './Admin').then(m => ...);
// SplitChunksPlugin: vendors, common, async chunks
Production example — Amazon

Context: Seller Central webpack splitChunks vendor + route bundles — admin analytics separate chunk.

Why they chose this approach: Route-level dynamic import in legacy Webpack app.

  • Pros: Initial login bundle small
  • Pros: Cache vendor chunk long-term
  • Cons: Chunk 404 if wrong publicPath on deploy
  • Cons: Many chunks on HTTP/1.1 — mitigated by HTTP/2
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
6. webpack-dev-server vs production build differences.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Dev: in-memory bundles, HMR, source maps cheap, no minification.
  2. Prod: minify, tree-shake, contenthash, split chunks, no HMR.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Dev: in-memory bundles, HMR, source maps cheap, no minification. Prod: minify, tree-shake, contenthash, split chunks, no HMR.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: webpack-dev-server vs production build differences..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
7. Source maps strategies for production.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

hidden-source-map or nosources-source-map — upload to Sentry, don't expose sources publicly.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

hidden-source-map or nosources-source-map — upload to Sentry, don't expose sources publicly.

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Source maps strategies for production..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP

resolve: { alias: { '@': path.resolve('src') }, extensions: ['.tsx', '.ts', '.js'] }

9. Asset modules (Webpack 5).
{ test: /\.png/, type: 'asset/resource' } // emits file
{ test: /\.svg/, type: 'asset/inline' }   // data URI

10. Bundle analyzer — finding bloat.
const BundleAnalyzerPlugin = require('webpack-bundle-analyzer').BundleAnalyzerPlugin;
plugins: [new BundleAnalyzerPlugin()]
Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Bundle analyzer — finding bloat..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
11. Loader execution order
Reasoning Loaders run right-to-left (or bottom-to-top in use array). sass-loader → css-loader → style-loader transforms SCSS to JS-injected styles.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Loaders run right-to-left (or bottom-to-top in use array).
  2. sass-loader → css-loader → style-loader transforms SCSS to JS-injected styles.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
use: ['style-loader', 'css-loader', 'sass-loader']
Trade-off style-loader in prod bloats JS — use MiniCssExtractPlugin in production.

Rule oneOf for different file types.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Loader execution order.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
12. babel-loader vs ts-loader
Reasoning babel-loader transpiles via Babel presets; ts-loader uses TypeScript compiler — can typecheck or transpileOnly + fork-ts-checker for speed.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

babel-loader transpiles via Babel presets; ts-loader uses TypeScript compiler — can typecheck or transpileOnly + fork-ts-checker for speed.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
ts-loader: { transpileOnly: true }
Trade-off esbuild-loader faster transpile without types — tsc --noEmit in CI for types.

Match team CI strategy.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: babel-loader vs ts-loader.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
13. thread-loader parallelization
Reasoning Offloads expensive loaders to worker pool — speeds large codebase builds.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Offloads expensive loaders to worker pool — speeds large codebase builds.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
use: [{ loader: 'thread-loader' }, { loader: 'babel-loader', options: { cacheDirectory: true } }]
Trade-off Worker startup overhead — not worth tiny projects.

Combine with cacheDirectory.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: thread-loader parallelization.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper

  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
14. Webpack 5 filesystem cache
Reasoning cache: { type: 'filesystem' } persists module graph between builds — dramatic CI speed with cache restore.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

cache: { type: 'filesystem' } persists module graph between builds — dramatic CI speed with cache restore.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
cache: { type: 'filesystem', buildDependencies: { config: [__filename] } }
Trade-off Stale cache after config change — buildDependencies invalidates.

Share cache in CI with actions/cache.

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Webpack 5 filesystem cache.

  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks

15. externals configuration
Reasoning Exclude deps from bundle — expect global on page (CDN React).
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Exclude deps from bundle — expect global on page (CDN React).

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
externals: { react: 'React' }
Trade-off Module Federation uses shared not externals for MFE.

Rare in modern SPA — prefer bundling for version control.

16. HotModuleReplacement API
Reasoning module.hot.accept('./App', () => render()) — swap module without full reload preserving state when possible.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

module.hot.accept('./App', () => render()) — swap module without full reload preserving state when possible.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
if (module.hot) module.hot.accept()
Trade-off Doesn't HMR failed syntax errors — full reload.

Vite HMR faster via ESM.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: HotModuleReplacement API.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

17. DefinePlugin / EnvironmentPlugin
Reasoning Replace process.env.NODE_ENV at build time — dead code elimination in production.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Replace process.env.NODE_ENV at build time — dead code elimination in production.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
new webpack.DefinePlugin({ 'process.env.API': JSON.stringify(process.env.API) })
Trade-off Leaks if accidentally embedding secrets — only public vars.

Vite uses import.meta.env instead.

18. Webpack 5 removed Node polyfills
Reasoning No auto polyfill for crypto/stream — add resolve.fallback if library needs Node APIs in browser.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

No auto polyfill for crypto/stream — add resolve.fallback if library needs Node APIs in browser.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
resolve: { fallback: { crypto: require.resolve('crypto-browserify') } }
Trade-off Prefer isomorphic library over polyfills.

Question signals webpack migration pain.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Webpack 5 removed Node polyfills.

19. MiniCssExtractPlugin vs style-loader
Reasoning Extract CSS to separate files in prod — parallel download, cacheable, no FOUC from JS CSS injection.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Extract CSS to separate files in prod — parallel download, cacheable, no FOUC from JS CSS injection.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
new MiniCssExtractPlugin({ filename: '[name].[contenthash].css' })
Trade-off More files to manage — HTTP/2 mitigates.

Critical CSS still separate concern.

css-loader modules: { localIdentName: '[name]__[local]___[hash:base64:5]' } — scoped class names.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

import styles from './Button.module.css'
Trade-off Global styles need :global selector.

22. Duplicate package detection
Reasoning Two versions of react in bundle — invalid hook call. webpack resolve.alias force single copy.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Two versions of react in bundle — invalid hook call.
  2. webpack resolve.alias force single copy.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
resolve: { alias: { react: path.resolve('./node_modules/react') } }
Trade-off pnpm strictness surfaces this earlier than npm hoisting.

npm ls react / why-dupes tools.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Duplicate package detection.

23. performance.maxAssetSize hints
Reasoning Warn or errors when entry/chunk exceeds budget — enforce perf in CI.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Warn or errors when entry/chunk exceeds budget — enforce perf in CI.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
performance: { maxEntrypointSize: 250000, hints: 'error' }
Trade-off Gzip size ≠ transferred brotli size — use size-limit on gzip.

Pair with bundle analyzer.

25. Webpack vs Rollup vs esbuild
Reasoning Webpack: app bundling, code splitting, ecosystem. Rollup: ESM libraries tree-shake. esbuild: speed transpile/bundle subset.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Webpack: app bundling, code splitting, ecosystem.
  2. Rollup: ESM libraries tree-shake.
  3. esbuild: speed transpile/bundle subset.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
Vite uses esbuild dev + Rollup prod
Trade-off Pick tool for job — senior knows migration paths.

esbuild doesn't replace all webpack loaders yet.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Webpack vs Rollup vs esbuild.

Reasoning apply(compiler) hooks into compilation lifecycle — emit assets, analyze bundle.

apply(compiler) hooks into compilation lifecycle — emit assets, analyze bundle.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
compiler.hooks.emit.tapAsync('MyPlugin', (compilation, cb) =&gt; { ...; cb(); })
Trade-off Prefer existing plugins before custom.

tap vs tapAsync vs tapPromise.
