27. Module resolution failure debugging
Reasoning Can't resolve module — wrong alias, missing dep, ESM/CJS mismatch, exports field in package.json.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Can't resolve module — wrong alias, missing dep, ESM/CJS mismatch, exports field in package.json.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Check resolve.extensions, mainFields: ['browser', 'module', 'main']
Trade-off pnpm .npmrc shamefully-hoist for broken packages.

Read error stack for resolver path.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Module resolution failure debugging.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
28. SplitChunksPlugin strategy
Reasoning Split vendors, common async chunks — cache groups by test regex.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Split vendors, common async chunks — cache groups by test regex.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
optimization: { splitChunks: { chunks: 'all', cacheGroups: { vendor: { test: /node_modules/ } } } }
Trade-off Over-splitting hurts HTTP/1.1 — less issue on H2.

Analyze before tuning defaults.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Seller Central webpack splitChunks vendor + route bundles — admin analytics separate chunk.

Why they chose this approach: Route-level dynamic import in legacy Webpack app.

  • Pros: Initial login bundle small
  • Pros: Cache vendor chunk long-term
  • Cons: Chunk 404 if wrong publicPath on deploy
  • Cons: Many chunks on HTTP/1.1 — mitigated by HTTP/2
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
29. Source map dev vs prod
Reasoning eval-cheap-module-source-map dev fast; hidden-source-map prod for Sentry without exposing publicly.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

eval-cheap-module-source-map dev fast; hidden-source-map prod for Sentry without exposing publicly.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
devtool: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production' ? 'hidden-source-map' : 'eval-cheap-module-source-map'
Trade-off Full source-map slow builds.

Upload maps to Sentry in CI only.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Source map dev vs prod.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
30. Webpack to Vite migration checklist
Reasoning index.html entry root, import.meta.env, no require(), vitest, check Node polyfills, update plugins to vite equivalents.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

index.html entry root, import.meta.env, no require(), vitest, check Node polyfills, update plugins to vite equivalents.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Manual test: dev HMR, prod build, dynamic imports, env vars
Trade-off Parallel run both in CI during transition.

Document in ADR for interview story.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Webpack to Vite migration checklist.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Vite equivalent? Map loader→plugin, splitChunks→rollup manualChunks, DefinePlugin→define, resolve.alias same in vite.config.
  • ESM-only migration? Drop require(), fix CJS deps, use "type":"module" or dual package — watch Node native fetch vs polyfills.
  • Monorepo debug? pnpm workspace protocol, transpilePackages in Next, trace resolution with DEBUG=vite:* or webpack stats.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

Vite (25 questions)

1. Why is Vite dev server faster than Webpack dev?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Dev: native ESM — browser requests modules directly, esbuild pre-bundles deps only.
  2. Webpack bundles entire graph before serve.
  3. HMR updates single module chain.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Dev: native ESM — browser requests modules directly, esbuild pre-bundles deps only. Webpack bundles entire graph before serve. HMR updates single module chain.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Seller dashboard migrated to Vite — dev server start 45s → 3s vs Webpack.

Why they chose this approach: Strangler migration with vite build parity gate.

  • Pros: ESM-native HMR instant on save
  • Pros: Smaller config surface
  • Cons: CJS deps break until fixed
  • Cons: MF still on Webpack for some remotes
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Still Webpack? Legacy IE, custom loader ecosystem, Module Federation host not on Vite yet — be honest about team constraints.
  • Dev vs prod? Dev: esbuild transform, no bundle; Prod: Rollup tree-shake — behavior differs for dynamic import and env vars.
  • CI validation? vite build + bundle size limit, preview smoke test, typecheck separate from transform-only dev server.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. Vite production build — what runs under the hood?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. vite build uses Rollup for tree-shaking, code splitting, minification (esbuild or terser).
  2. Not esbuild for full prod bundle historically — Rollup for better tree-shake.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

vite build uses Rollup for tree-shaking, code splitting, minification (esbuild or terser). Not esbuild for full prod bundle historically — Rollup for better tree-shake.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Vite production build — what runs under the hood?.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Still Webpack? Legacy IE, custom loader ecosystem, Module Federation host not on Vite yet — be honest about team constraints.
  • Dev vs prod? Dev: esbuild transform, no bundle; Prod: Rollup tree-shake — behavior differs for dynamic import and env vars.
  • CI validation? vite build + bundle size limit, preview smoke test, typecheck separate from transform-only dev server.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. vite.config.ts essential options.
export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [react()],
  base: '/my-app/',
  resolve: { alias: { '@': '/src' } },
  server: { port: 5173, proxy: { '/api': 'http://localhost:3000' } },
  build: { target: 'es2020', sourcemap: true, rollupOptions: { output: { manualChunks: { vendor: ['react', 'react-dom'] } } } }
});
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: vite.config.ts essential options..

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Still Webpack? Legacy IE, custom loader ecosystem, Module Federation host not on Vite yet — be honest about team constraints.
  • Dev vs prod? Dev: esbuild transform, no bundle; Prod: Rollup tree-shake — behavior differs for dynamic import and env vars.
  • CI validation? vite build + bundle size limit, preview smoke test, typecheck separate from transform-only dev server.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. Environment variables in Vite.
// .env.local — VITE_ prefix exposed to client
VITE_API_URL=https://api.example.com
// code: import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL
// NOT process.env (unless defined) — security by prefix
Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Environment variables in Vite..

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Still Webpack? Legacy IE, custom loader ecosystem, Module Federation host not on Vite yet — be honest about team constraints.
  • Dev vs prod? Dev: esbuild transform, no bundle; Prod: Rollup tree-shake — behavior differs for dynamic import and env vars.
  • CI validation? vite build + bundle size limit, preview smoke test, typecheck separate from transform-only dev server.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. SSR with Vite.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. vite build --ssr + server entry.
  2. Frameworks: vite-plugin-ssr, TanStack Start.
  3. Dual bundle client + server.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

vite build --ssr + server entry. Frameworks: vite-plugin-ssr, TanStack Start. Dual bundle client + server.

// vite.config.ts

Shares resolve aliases, plugins, transforms — tests match app bundling.

7. optimizeDeps — pre-bundling.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. esbuild bundles CJS deps to ESM on first dev start.
  2. Force re-scan: optimizeDeps.force .
  3. Include/exclude problematic packages.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

esbuild bundles CJS deps to ESM on first dev start. Force re-scan: optimizeDeps.force. Include/exclude problematic packages.

10. Migrating Webpack → Vite checklist.
  • Replace require → import
  • index.html at root entry
  • import.meta.env not process.env
  • SVG/React — vite-plugin-svgr
  • Node polyfills — rarely needed
  • Jest → Vitest

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Migrating Webpack → Vite checklist..

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity

  • Still Webpack? Legacy IE, custom loader ecosystem, Module Federation host not on Vite yet — be honest about team constraints.

11. Vite HMR API import.meta.hot
Reasoning accept hot updates for module without full reload; dispose cleanup on update.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

accept hot updates for module without full reload; dispose cleanup on update.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
if (import.meta.hot) { import.meta.hot.accept(); import.meta.hot.dispose(() =&gt; cleanup()); }
Trade-off Full reload if boundary can't accept — check console.

Framework plugins handle most HMR.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Seller dashboard migrated to Vite — dev server start 45s → 3s vs Webpack.

Why they chose this approach: Strangler migration with vite build parity gate.

  • Pros: ESM-native HMR instant on save
  • Pros: Smaller config surface
  • Cons: CJS deps break until fixed
  • Cons: MF still on Webpack for some remotes
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Still Webpack? Legacy IE, custom loader ecosystem, Module Federation host not on Vite yet — be honest about team constraints.
  • Dev vs prod? Dev: esbuild transform, no bundle; Prod: Rollup tree-shake — behavior differs for dynamic import and env vars.
  • CI validation? vite build + bundle size limit, preview smoke test, typecheck separate from transform-only dev server.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
12. CSS code splitting in Vite build
Reasoning Async chunk imports get separate CSS files extracted by Rollup — loaded when chunk loads.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Async chunk imports get separate CSS files extracted by Rollup — loaded when chunk loads.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const Chart = lazy(() =&gt; import('./Chart.css' implicit via import))
Trade-off FOUC if CSS chunk slow — preload critical.

Same as webpack mini-css-extract behavior.

  • Dev vs prod? Dev: esbuild transform, no bundle; Prod: Rollup tree-shake — behavior differs for dynamic import and env vars.

Reasoning postcss.config.js auto-loaded — tailwind, autoprefixer without extra vite config.

postcss.config.js auto-loaded — tailwind, autoprefixer without extra vite config.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
export default { plugins: { tailwindcss: {}, autoprefixer: {} } }

Tailwind v4 vite plugin alternative.

14. Tailwind with Vite
Reasoning @tailwindcss/vite plugin or classic postcss tailwind — content paths scan all template files.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

@tailwindcss/vite plugin or classic postcss tailwind — content paths scan all template files.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
content: ['./index.html', './src/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx}']
Trade-off Missing content path = empty CSS output.

JIT generates only used utilities.

15. Vite plugin order
Reasoning React plugin before plugins that transform JSX; enforce plugin if ordering critical.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

React plugin before plugins that transform JSX; enforce plugin if ordering critical.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
plugins: [react(), svgr(), legacy()]
Trade-off Wrong order = cryptic transform errors.

inspect transformed id in debug.

16. define config constants
Reasoning Replace global constants at build — like DefinePlugin.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Replace global constants at build — like DefinePlugin.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
define: { __APP_VERSION__: JSON.stringify(process.env.npm_package_version) }
Trade-off Don't embed secrets.

Use import.meta.env for env vars primarily.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: define config constants.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Still Webpack? Legacy IE, custom loader ecosystem, Module Federation host not on Vite yet — be honest about team constraints.
  • Dev vs prod? Dev: esbuild transform, no bundle; Prod: Rollup tree-shake — behavior differs for dynamic import and env vars.
  • CI validation? vite build + bundle size limit, preview smoke test, typecheck separate from transform-only dev server.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
17. public/ directory behavior
Reasoning Files copied as-is to dist root — no hashing. Good favicon, robots.txt.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Files copied as-is to dist root — no hashing.
  2. Good favicon, robots.txt.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
public/favicon.ico → /favicon.ico
Trade-off Large assets in public skip cache busting — prefer imported assets for hashing.

Reference as /favicon.ico not import.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: public/ directory behavior.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Still Webpack? Legacy IE, custom loader ecosystem, Module Federation host not on Vite yet — be honest about team constraints.
  • Dev vs prod? Dev: esbuild transform, no bundle; Prod: Rollup tree-shake — behavior differs for dynamic import and env vars.
  • CI validation? vite build + bundle size limit, preview smoke test, typecheck separate from transform-only dev server.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
18. assetsInlineLimit
Reasoning Assets smaller than threshold inlined as base64 data URL in JS.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Assets smaller than threshold inlined as base64 data URL in JS.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
build: { assetsInlineLimit: 4096 }
Trade-off Inlined assets bloat JS — increase limit carefully for icons.

SVG as component via vite-plugin-svgr instead.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: assetsInlineLimit.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Still Webpack? Legacy IE, custom loader ecosystem, Module Federation host not on Vite yet — be honest about team constraints.
  • Dev vs prod? Dev: esbuild transform, no bundle; Prod: Rollup tree-shake — behavior differs for dynamic import and env vars.
  • CI validation? vite build + bundle size limit, preview smoke test, typecheck separate from transform-only dev server.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
19. vite preview command
Reasoning Serves production dist locally — verify prod build before deploy.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Serves production dist locally — verify prod build before deploy.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
pnpm build && pnpm preview --port 4173
Trade-off Not for production serving — use nginx/CDN.

Catch base path misconfig before GitHub Pages deploy.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: vite preview command.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity

  • Dev vs prod? Dev: esbuild transform, no bundle; Prod: Rollup tree-shake — behavior differs for dynamic import and env vars.

20. Vite mode and env files
Reasoning .env, .env.local, .env.[mode], .env.[mode].local — loaded by mode flag.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

.env, .env.local, .env.[mode], .env.[mode].local — loaded by mode flag.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
vite build --mode staging loads .env.staging
Trade-off .env.local gitignored for secrets.

loadEnv in vite.config for config-time vars.

21. loadEnv in vite.config
Reasoning Access env in config for conditional plugins.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Access env in config for conditional plugins.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const env = loadEnv(mode, process.cwd(), '');
Trade-off Third param prefix filter — empty loads all (careful secrets).

Only VITE_ exposed to client code automatically.

23. Monorepo Vite setup
Reasoning Workspace packages linked — resolve alias to source for fast HMR across packages.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Workspace packages linked — resolve alias to source for fast HMR across packages.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
resolve: { alias: { '@org/ui': path.resolve(__dirname, '../packages/ui/src') } }
Trade-off Build order — depend on ^build in turbo.

publish packages before app consumes npm version.

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Monorepo Vite setup.

24. server.warmup (Vite 5+)
Reasoning Pre-transform frequently imported files on server start — faster first page load dev.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Pre-transform frequently imported files on server start — faster first page load dev.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
server: { warmup: { clientFiles: ['./src/main.tsx', './src/App.tsx'] } }
Trade-off Over-warmup slows server start.

DX optimization for large apps.

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: server.warmup (Vite 5+).

  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks

Reasoning Rust-based bundler may replace Rollup for prod builds — faster builds same config direction.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Watch Vite changelog / rolldown-vite experiments
Trade-off Migration should stay config-compatible in vision.

Mention awareness — shows you follow tooling.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.

HTML (30 questions)

1. Semantic HTML — why it matters for a11y and SEO.
<header><nav><main><article><section><aside><footer>
// Not <div class="header"> everywhere — landmarks for screen readers
Production example — Walmart

Context: Checkout semantic form labels + aria-live on price update for screen readers.

Why they chose this approach: WCAG 2.1 AA on checkout — legal and conversion for aging customers.

  • Pros: Fewer support calls on payment errors
  • Pros: Keyboard-complete checkout
  • Cons: Custom widgets need manual a11y testing
  • Cons: ARIA misuse worse than none
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. Document loading — defer vs async scripts.
  • async: download parallel, execute when ready — order not guaranteed
  • defer: parallel download, execute after HTML parsed — order preserved
  • Module scripts defer by default
<script type="module" src="/app.js"></script>
Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Document loading — defer vs async scripts..

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. Critical rendering path optimization.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Minimize blocking CSS/JS, inline critical CSS, preload fonts, defer non-critical JS, reduce DOM depth, avoid layout thrashing.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Minimize blocking CSS/JS, inline critical CSS, preload fonts, defer non-critical JS, reduce DOM depth, avoid layout thrashing.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Critical rendering path optimization..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. Accessibility: ARIA roles — first rule of ARIA.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. "Don't use ARIA if native element exists." <button> not <div role="button"> .
  2. ARIA supplements, doesn't fix bad HTML.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

"Don't use ARIA if native element exists." <button> not <div role="button">. ARIA supplements, doesn't fix bad HTML.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Checkout semantic form labels + aria-live on price update for screen readers.

Why they chose this approach: WCAG 2.1 AA on checkout — legal and conversion for aging customers.

  • Pros: Fewer support calls on payment errors
  • Pros: Keyboard-complete checkout
  • Cons: Custom widgets need manual a11y testing
  • Cons: ARIA misuse worse than none
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. Form accessibility patterns.
<label for="email">Email</label>
<input id="email" type="email" aria-describedby="email-hint" aria-invalid={!!error} />
<span id="email-hint">Work email only</span>
{error && <span role="alert">{error}</span>}
Production example — Walmart

Context: Checkout semantic form labels + aria-live on price update for screen readers.

Why they chose this approach: WCAG 2.1 AA on checkout — legal and conversion for aging customers.

  • Pros: Fewer support calls on payment errors
  • Pros: Keyboard-complete checkout
  • Cons: Custom widgets need manual a11y testing
  • Cons: ARIA misuse worse than none
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
6. Content Security Policy (CSP).
Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'nonce-abc'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'

Prevents XSS injection execution. nonce/hash for inline scripts in SSR.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Content Security Policy (CSP)..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
7. Shadow DOM vs Light DOM.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Shadow DOM encapsulates styles/markup — Web Components.
  2. React doesn't use Shadow by default — libraries like @lit-labs/react bridge.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Shadow DOM encapsulates styles/markup — Web Components. React doesn't use Shadow by default — libraries like @lit-labs/react bridge.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Shadow DOM vs Light DOM..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
11. DOCTYPE and standards mode
Reasoning DOCTYPE html triggers standards mode — not quirks mode with wrong box model behavior.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

DOCTYPE html triggers standards mode — not quirks mode with wrong box model behavior.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<!DOCTYPE html>
Trade-off Missing DOCTYPE breaks layout subtly in old IE — still teach concept.

First line of every HTML doc.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: DOCTYPE and standards mode.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
12. lang attribute on html
Reasoning Screen readers pick voice; hyphenation; translation tools. SEO minor signal.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Screen readers pick voice; hyphenation; translation tools.
  2. SEO minor signal.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
<html lang="en">
Trade-off Wrong lang hurts a11y more than SEO.

Required in WCAG for valid page.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: lang attribute on html.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
13. tabindex values
Reasoning 0 = natural tab order. -1 = programmatic focus only (skip link target). Positive tabindex anti-pattern — scrambles order.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. 0 = natural tab order.
  2. -1 = programmatic focus only (skip link target).
  3. Positive tabindex anti-pattern — scrambles order.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
<main id="main" tabindex="-1">
Trade-off Positive tabindex harms keyboard users.

Manage focus in modals with -1 roving tabindex.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: tabindex values.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
14. Focus trap in modal
Reasoning Tab cycles within dialog until closed; restore focus to trigger on close.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Tab cycles within dialog until closed; restore focus to trigger on close.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
useDialogFocus hook: query focusable elements, handle Tab wrap
Trade-off Inert attribute on background simpler in modern browsers.

WCAG 2.4.3 focus order.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Focus trap in modal.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
15. inert attribute
Reasoning Native disable interaction on siblings outside modal — pointer-events + a11y tree exclusion.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Native disable interaction on siblings outside modal — pointer-events + a11y tree exclusion.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<div inert aria-hidden="true">background</div>
Trade-off Polyfill for older Safari if needed.

Prefer over aria-hidden alone on wrong nodes.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: inert attribute.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.

  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.

16. dialog element native modal
Reasoning showModal() top layer, ::backdrop, Escape close, focus management built-in.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

showModal() top layer, ::backdrop, Escape close, focus management built-in.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<dialog ref={d}><form method="dialog">...</form></dialog>
Trade-off Style backdrop with ::backdrop pseudo.

React: ref.showModal() in effect.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: dialog element native modal.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.

17. details/summary accordion
Reasoning Native disclosure widget — no JS required for expand/collapse.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Native disclosure widget — no JS required for expand/collapse.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<details><summary>More</summary>content</details>
Trade-off Limited styling vs custom accordion.

Good progressive enhancement.

18. input type benefits
Reasoning email, tel, url, number trigger appropriate mobile keyboards and basic validation.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

email, tel, url, number trigger appropriate mobile keyboards and basic validation.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<input type="email" autocomplete="email" />
Trade-off type=number spinner UX issues for IDs — sometimes text + pattern.

autocomplete helps password managers.

19. autocomplete attributes
Reasoning Help browsers/password managers fill correctly — reduce friction and errors.

Help browsers/password managers fill correctly — reduce friction and errors.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
autocomplete="current-password"

Follow WHATWG autocomplete table.

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: autocomplete attributes.

20. CSP report-uri / report-to
Reasoning Monitor XSS/injection attempts via reporting endpoint.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Monitor XSS/injection attempts via reporting endpoint.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only: default-src 'self'; report-uri /csp-report
Trade-off Report-Only mode test before enforce.

Nonce scripts in SSR apps.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: CSP report-uri / report-to.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity

21. Subresource Integrity SRI
Reasoning Browser verifies hash of CDN script before execute — tamper detection.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Browser verifies hash of CDN script before execute — tamper detection.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<script src="cdn.com/lib.js" integrity="sha384-..." crossorigin="anonymous">
Trade-off Hash must update when CDN file changes.

Required for security-conscious orgs.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Subresource Integrity SRI.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
22. preload vs prefetch vs preconnect
Reasoning preload: current page critical. prefetch: likely next navigation low priority. preconnect: early connection setup.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. preload: current page critical.
  2. prefetch: likely next navigation low priority.
  3. preconnect: early connection setup.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
<link rel="preload" href="/hero.webp" as="image">
Trade-off Too many preloads compete with LCP.

See journeys section for full narrative.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: preload vs prefetch vs preconnect.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
23. iframe sandbox
Reasoning Restrict iframe capabilities — scripts, forms, top navigation.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Restrict iframe capabilities — scripts, forms, top navigation.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<iframe sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin">
Trade-off Over-restrict breaks embed functionality.

Third-party widgets sometimes need sandbox.

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: iframe sandbox.

24. postMessage cross-origin
Reasoning Structured communication between windows/iframes — validate origin always.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Structured communication between windows/iframes — validate origin always.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
if (e.origin !== 'https://trusted.com') return;
Trade-off Wildcard targetOrigin "*" dangerous.

MFE iframe pattern.

25. Web Components slots
Reasoning Light DOM content projected into shadow component slots.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Light DOM content projected into shadow component slots.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<my-card><span slot="title">Hi</span></my-card>
Trade-off React slot story different — use children/props.

Design systems may wrap web components.

26. template element
Reasoning Inert HTML cloneable by JS — not rendered until activated.

Inert HTML cloneable by JS — not rendered until activated.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const t = document.querySelector("#row"); tbody.append(t.content.cloneNode(true));
Trade-off Table row templates common pattern.

Not replaced by JSX but same idea.

27. JSON-LD structured data
Reasoning Search engines parse schema.org JSON in script type application/ld+json.

Search engines parse schema.org JSON in script type application/ld+json.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<script type="application/ld+json">{"@type":"Product",...}</script>
Trade-off Invalid JSON-LD ignored silently — test in Google Rich Results.

SPA must inject or SSR JSON-LD.

  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
29. Hydration in HTML/SSR context
Reasoning Server sends HTML; client JS attaches event listeners and state — must match.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Server sends HTML; client JS attaches event listeners and state — must match.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Mismatch warning in React 18+
Trade-off Fix data before blaming framework.

Tied to SSR architecture interviews.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Hydration in HTML/SSR context.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
30. Progressive enhancement baseline
Reasoning Core content works without JS; enhance with JS for interactivity.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Core content works without JS; enhance with JS for interactivity.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<form action="/search" method="get"><input name="q"></form>
Trade-off SPAs often fail without JS — acknowledge trade-off.

Senior: when PE matters (gov, emerging markets).

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Progressive enhancement baseline.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • A11y requirement? WCAG 2.1 AA typical — keyboard, contrast, labels, live regions; cite success criterion if you know it.
  • SSR change? Hydration must match; noscript fallbacks; meta tags in streamed HTML head — don't rely on client-only title.
  • SEO? Semantic headings, canonical, structured data, crawlable links — SPA needs SSR/SSG for critical content.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

CSS (35 questions)

1. CSS specificity calculation.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Inline (1000) > ID (100) > Class/attr/pseudo-class (10) > Element (1).
  2. !important overrides cascade.
  3. :where() zero specificity useful for resets.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Inline (1000) > ID (100) > Class/attr/pseudo-class (10) > Element (1). !important overrides cascade. :where() zero specificity useful for resets.

:where(.card, .panel) { margin: 0; } /* specificity 0 — easy override */
Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: CSS specificity calculation..

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Design system? Tokens in CSS variables, lint with stylelint, document in Storybook, enforce via PR checklist.
  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. BFC (Block Formatting Context) — triggers and use.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Triggers: float, position absolute/fixed, overflow not visible, display flow-root, flex/grid item.
  2. Contains margin collapse, clears floats.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Triggers: float, position absolute/fixed, overflow not visible, display flow-root, flex/grid item. Contains margin collapse, clears floats.

.clearfix { display: flow-root; }
Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: BFC (Block Formatting Context) — triggers and use..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Design system? Tokens in CSS variables, lint with stylelint, document in Storybook, enforce via PR checklist.
  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. Flexbox vs Grid — decision matrix.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Flex: 1D layout, alignment, nav bars, equal height columns.
  2. Grid: 2D, page layouts, complex areas.
  3. Often combine — grid page, flex components.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Flex: 1D layout, alignment, nav bars, equal height columns. Grid: 2D, page layouts, complex areas. Often combine — grid page, flex components.

.grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(280px, 1fr)); gap: 1rem; }
Production example — Myntra

Context: PLP CSS Grid 2-col mobile 4-col desktop; container queries on product card.

Why they chose this approach: Mobile-first breakpoints + logical properties for RTL markets.

  • Pros: Consistent fashion grid across devices
  • Pros: Dark mode via CSS variables
  • Cons: Legacy browsers need fallbacks
  • Cons: Grid + subgrid support varies
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Design system? Tokens in CSS variables, lint with stylelint, document in Storybook, enforce via PR checklist.
  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. CSS Grid subgrid.
.parent { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); }
.child { display: grid; grid-column: span 3; grid-template-columns: subgrid; }

Nested items align to parent tracks — card layouts.

Production example — Myntra

Context: PLP CSS Grid 2-col mobile 4-col desktop; container queries on product card.

Why they chose this approach: Mobile-first breakpoints + logical properties for RTL markets.

  • Pros: Consistent fashion grid across devices
  • Pros: Dark mode via CSS variables
  • Cons: Legacy browsers need fallbacks
  • Cons: Grid + subgrid support varies
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Design system? Tokens in CSS variables, lint with stylelint, document in Storybook, enforce via PR checklist.
  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. Container queries vs media queries.
.card { container-type: inline-size; }
@container (min-width: 400px) { .card__title { font-size: 1.5rem; } }

Component responds to parent width — true component-driven responsive design.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: Container queries vs media queries..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP

  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.

6. CSS custom properties (variables) theming.
:root { --color-primary: #2563eb; }
[data-theme="dark"] { --color-primary: #60a5fa; }
.btn { background: var(--color-primary); }

Cascade + runtime change unlike Sass vars.

7. Stacking context and z-index bugs.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. z-index only compares within same stacking context.
  2. New context: opacity < 1, transform, filter, isolation: isolate, positioned + z-index.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

z-index only compares within same stacking context. New context: opacity < 1, transform, filter, isolation: isolate, positioned + z-index.

10. CSS Modules vs CSS-in-JS vs Tailwind — trade-offs.
  • Modules: scoped classes, zero runtime
  • CSS-in-JS (styled-components): dynamic props, runtime cost, RSC issues
  • Tailwind: utility speed, design tokens, purge/tree-shake
  • Trend: zero-runtime — Modules, Tailwind, vanilla-extract, Panda CSS
Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: CSS Modules vs CSS-in-JS vs Tailwind — trade-offs..

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

Reasoning width includes padding+border — intuitive layout math.

width includes padding+border — intuitive layout math.

Example
*, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box; }
Trade-off Rare exceptions for third-party widgets.

Standard reset in modern apps.

12. margin collapse
Reasoning Vertical adjacent margins merge into one — unexpected gaps.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Vertical adjacent margins merge into one — unexpected gaps.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Parent padding or border or flow-root prevents collapse with child.
Trade-off Flex/grid items don't collapse margins same way.

Debug "mystery gap" interviews.

13. position sticky requirements
Reasoning Needs threshold (top:0), ancestor without overflow:hidden/auto breaking stick.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Needs threshold (top:0), ancestor without overflow:hidden/auto breaking stick.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
.header { position: sticky; top: 0; }
Trade-off Sticky inside overflow hidden fails silently.

Common bug in modals/tables.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example

Design token friendly.

18. object-fit and object-position
Reasoning Image in fixed box — cover crops, contain letterboxes.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Image in fixed box — cover crops, contain letterboxes.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
img { width:100%; height:200px; object-fit: cover; }
Trade-off Background-image no alt text — prefer img for content.

Avatar thumbnails standard.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: object-fit and object-position.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Design system? Tokens in CSS variables, lint with stylelint, document in Storybook, enforce via PR checklist.
  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
19. will-change hint
Reasoning Hints browser to promote layer — transform, opacity.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Hints browser to promote layer — transform, opacity.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
will-change: transform;
Trade-off Overuse increases memory — apply before animation remove after.

Don't will-change everything preemptively.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: will-change hint.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Design system? Tokens in CSS variables, lint with stylelint, document in Storybook, enforce via PR checklist.
  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
20. contain property
Reasoning Isolates layout/paint/style — perf optimization for widgets.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Isolates layout/paint/style — perf optimization for widgets.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
contain: layout paint;
Trade-off Wrong contain clips overflow unexpectedly.

Useful for complex list items.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: contain property.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Design system? Tokens in CSS variables, lint with stylelint, document in Storybook, enforce via PR checklist.
  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
21. content-visibility auto
Reasoning Browser skips rendering off-screen — huge lists perf.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Browser skips rendering off-screen — huge lists perf.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
.row { content-visibility: auto; contain-intrinsic-size: 0 80px; }
Trade-off Intrinsic size estimate needed to avoid scrollbar jump.

Complements virtualization.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: content-visibility auto.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Design system? Tokens in CSS variables, lint with stylelint, document in Storybook, enforce via PR checklist.
  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
22. @layer cascade layers
Reasoning @layer reset, base, components, utilities — explicit precedence without !important wars.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

@layer reset, base, components, utilities — explicit precedence without !important wars.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
@layer utilities { .hidden { display: none !important; } }
Trade-off Unlayered CSS beats layered — watch import order.

Tailwind v4 uses layers heavily.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: @layer cascade layers.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)

  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.

23. @property registered custom props
Reasoning Animate CSS variables with typed registration.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Animate CSS variables with typed registration.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
@property --angle { syntax: "<angle>"; inherits: false; initial-value: 0deg; }
Trade-off Limited browser support check.

Advanced animation interviews.

24. scroll-snap

Carousel/slider UX — mandatory snap points.

25. overscroll-behavior
Reasoning Prevent scroll chaining pull-to-refresh bleed in nested scroll areas.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Prevent scroll chaining pull-to-refresh bleed in nested scroll areas.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
overscroll-behavior: contain;
Trade-off Fix modal body scroll behind on iOS historically hard.

Drawer/modal patterns.

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: overscroll-behavior.

Reasoning Show focus ring keyboard not mouse — better UX.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Show focus ring keyboard not mouse — better UX.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--focus); }
Trade-off :focus { outline:none } without :focus-visible harms a11y.

Replace outline:none antipattern.

27. prefers-reduced-motion
Reasoning Respect user OS setting — disable non-essential animation.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Respect user OS setting — disable non-essential animation.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { * { animation: none !important; } }
Trade-off Essential motion (loading) still OK with subtlety.

Legal requirement some jurisdictions.

Reasoning Progressive enhancement — apply grid only if supported.

Progressive enhancement — apply grid only if supported.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
@supports (display: grid) { .layout { display: grid; } }
Trade-off Not substitute for baseline layout.

Graceful degradation narrative.

30. Critical CSS strategy
Reasoning Inline above-fold CSS in head; async load rest.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Inline above-fold CSS in head; async load rest.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<style>/* critical */</style><link rel="preload" href="rest.css" as="style" onload="...">
Trade-off Manual critical CSS maintenance cost.

Tools: critters, penthouse.
