Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Critical CSS strategy.

31. Sass @use vs @import
Reasoning @import deprecated — @use namespaced, loads once.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

@import deprecated — @use namespaced, loads once.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
@use "tokens/colors" as c; .btn { color: c.$primary; }
Trade-off Migrate before Dart Sass 3 removes @import.

Still common in legacy codebases.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Sass @use vs @import.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.
If the interviewer pushes deeper

33. View Transitions API
Reasoning document.startViewTransition(() => updateDOM) — animated SPA route changes.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

document.startViewTransition(() => updateDOM) — animated SPA route changes.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
if (document.startViewTransition) document.startViewTransition(() => render());
Trade-off Chrome-first — progressive enhancement.

Emerging UX pattern 2024-25.

35. INP and compositor-only animation
Reasoning INP sensitive to main thread — animate transform/opacity only for 60fps without blocking.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

INP sensitive to main thread — animate transform/opacity only for 60fps without blocking.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
transition: transform 200ms; /* not width/left */
Trade-off Layout-triggering animation hurts INP during interaction.

Link CSS perf to Core Web Vitals.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: INP and compositor-only animation.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Design system? Tokens in CSS variables, lint with stylelint, document in Storybook, enforce via PR checklist.
  • Tailwind equivalent? Give utility classes or @apply extraction — show you know both mental models.
  • RTL / dark mode? logical properties (margin-inline), :dir(rtl), prefers-color-scheme or data-theme — avoid hard-coded left/right.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

Architecture & System Design (40 questions)

1. Design a large-scale frontend architecture for 50 engineers.
Reasoning At this scale you need: clear ownership boundaries, shared platform (CI, DS, observability), monorepo or strong module federation, contract testing, and ADRs. Prevent every team inventing webpack config. Platform team enables; product teams ship features.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. At this scale you need: clear ownership boundaries, shared platform (CI, DS, observability), monorepo or strong module federation, contract testing, and ADRs.
  2. Prevent every team inventing webpack config.
  3. Platform team enables; product teams ship features.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
monorepo/
  apps/web, apps/admin
  packages/ui, tokens, eslint-config, api-client
  turbo.json pipeline: lint → test → build (cached)
  Chromatic + Playwright on critical paths
Trade-off Micro-frontends only if org boundaries justify ops cost — modular monolith + code split often enough until ~10 teams.

Close with metrics: deploy frequency, MTTR, bundle budget compliance %.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Design a large-scale frontend architecture for 50 engineers..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. Micro-frontends — when worth it vs modular monolith?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. MFE when: independent deploy teams, different tech stacks, scale org boundaries.
  2. Cost: shared deps, UX consistency, latency, integration testing.
  3. Modular monolith: single deploy, code-split routes — often enough until team >10 per domain.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

MFE when: independent deploy teams, different tech stacks, scale org boundaries. Cost: shared deps, UX consistency, latency, integration testing. Modular monolith: single deploy, code-split routes — often enough until team >10 per domain.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Micro-frontends — when worth it vs modular monolith?.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. State management architecture — client vs server state.
// Server state: TanStack Query / RTK Query — cache, staleTime, invalidation
// Client UI state: useState, Zustand — modals, filters
// URL state: searchParams — shareable bookmarkable
// Form state: React Hook Form — ephemeral

Anti-pattern: putting API cache in Redux manually in 2025.

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: State management architecture — client vs server state..

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. API layer design — repository pattern.
// lib/api/orders.ts
export async function fetchOrders(params: OrderParams): Promise<Order[]> {
  const res = await client.get('/orders', { params });
  return OrderSchema.array().parse(res.data); // runtime validation
}

UI never calls fetch directly — swap mock/real, centralize auth/errors.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile web BFF aggregates cart + offers + user in one round trip for checkout.

Why they chose this approach: GraphQL or REST BFF tailored to UI screens.

  • Pros: Fewer client round trips on 4G
  • Pros: Hide microservice sprawl
  • Cons: BFF becomes god service
  • Cons: Duplication across iOS/Android/web BFFs
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. Authentication architecture in SPA.
  • BFF pattern — HttpOnly refresh cookie
  • Access token in memory (short TTL)
  • PKCE for OAuth public clients
  • CSRF protection on cookie auth
  • Never store refresh in localStorage
  • Silent refresh + redirect on 401
Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Authentication architecture in SPA..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
6. CORS — explain preflight and fixes.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Cross-origin requests trigger OPTIONS preflight for non-simple requests.
  2. Server returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Methods, Headers.
  3. Fix: configure API CORS or same-origin proxy in dev (Vite proxy).

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Cross-origin requests trigger OPTIONS preflight for non-simple requests. Server returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Methods, Headers. Fix: configure API CORS or same-origin proxy in dev (Vite proxy).

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: CORS — explain preflight and fixes..

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
7. Web Vitals — LCP, INP, CLS targets and fixes.
  • LCP <2.5s: optimize hero image, SSR, CDN
  • INP <200ms: break up JS, web workers, defer third-party
  • CLS <0.1: size images, no layout shift ads
Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Web Vitals — LCP, INP, CLS targets and fixes..

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
8. Design system — tokens, components, documentation.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Tokens (color, spacing, typography) → primitives (Button, Input) → patterns (LoginForm).
  2. Storybook + visual regression (Chromatic).
  3. Semantic tokens for theming.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Tokens (color, spacing, typography) → primitives (Button, Input) → patterns (LoginForm). Storybook + visual regression (Chromatic). Semantic tokens for theming.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Design system — tokens, components, documentation..

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
9. Feature flags architecture.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. LaunchDarkly / Unleash / custom.
  2. Evaluate flags server-side when security-critical.
  3. Client flags for UI experiments.
  4. Avoid flag spaghetti — sunset flags.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

LaunchDarkly / Unleash / custom. Evaluate flags server-side when security-critical. Client flags for UI experiments. Avoid flag spaghetti — sunset flags.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Feature flags architecture..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

10. Error handling strategy — layers.
  • Boundary: React ErrorBoundary per route
  • API: normalized error type, toast user message
  • Global: Sentry capture with breadcrumbs
  • Retry: exponential backoff idempotent GETs
Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Error handling strategy — layers..

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

11. Testing pyramid for frontend at scale.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Many unit (utils, hooks, reducers) → integration (RTL + MSW) → fewer E2E (Playwright critical paths).
  2. Visual regression for design system.
  3. Contract tests for API.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Many unit (utils, hooks, reducers) → integration (RTL + MSW) → fewer E2E (Playwright critical paths). Visual regression for design system. Contract tests for API.

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Testing pyramid for frontend at scale..

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player

12. CI/CD pipeline for frontend.
# PR: lint → typecheck → unit → build → bundle size check
# main: + E2E staging → deploy preview → prod canary

Tools: GitHub Actions, Vercel/Netlify previews, semantic release.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: CI/CD pipeline for frontend..

  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks

13. Monorepo tooling — Nx vs Turborepo.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Both: task caching, affected builds.
  2. Nx: generators, module graph, enforced boundaries.
  3. Turborepo: simpler, pipeline focused.
  4. Share design-system, configs across apps.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Both: task caching, affected builds. Nx: generators, module graph, enforced boundaries. Turborepo: simpler, pipeline focused. Share design-system, configs across apps.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Monorepo tooling — Nx vs Turborepo..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

14. SSR vs SSG vs ISR vs CSR — choose per page.
  • SSG: marketing, docs — build time HTML
  • ISR: semi-static e-commerce listings
  • SSR: personalized, SEO dynamic
  • CSR: dashboards behind auth
Production example — Myntra

Context: Category PLP ISR revalidate 60s — sale hour drops to on-demand revalidate.

Why they chose this approach: Edge SSR for SEO PLP; CSR for account settings.

  • Pros: Google indexes sale categories
  • Pros: CDN serves stale while revalidating
  • Cons: Cache invalidation complexity on price drop
  • Cons: SSR cost at peak traffic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
15. Real-time architecture — WebSockets vs SSE vs polling.
  • WebSocket: bidirectional — chat, live GPS
  • SSE: server push one-way — notifications
  • Polling: simple, higher latency — 30s fleet map OK for demo
Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Real-time architecture — WebSockets vs SSE vs polling..

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player

16. Event-driven UI decoupling
Reasoning Custom events or message bus decouple modules — MFE shell doesn't import remote internals.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Custom events or message bus decouple modules — MFE shell doesn't import remote internals.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
bus.emit({ type: "CART_UPDATED", count })
Trade-off Global bus can become spaghetti — document event catalog.

Typed event schema in TypeScript.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Event-driven UI decoupling.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks

17. Idempotency keys frontend
Reasoning Client generates UUID once per user action — retry safe on network failure.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Client generates UUID once per user action — retry safe on network failure.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Idempotency-Key header on POST payment
Trade-off Server must store key window 24h.

Prevents double charge UX bug.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Idempotency keys frontend.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

18. Optimistic UI 409 rollback
Reasoning Version conflict from server — revert optimistic patch show merge UI.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Version conflict from server — revert optimistic patch show merge UI.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
if (res.status === 409) rollback(); showConflictDialog(serverState)
Trade-off Last-write-wins loses data.

Collaborative editing needs CRDT not optimistic alone.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Optimistic UI 409 rollback.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

Background Sync API or manual retry loop

20. CDN caching strategy
Reasoning Hashed assets immutable 1 year; index.html short cache or no-cache.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Hashed assets immutable 1 year; index.html short cache or no-cache.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable
Trade-off CDN purge on deploy for non-hashed assets.

CloudFront/Fastly interview names.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: CDN caching strategy.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks

  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
21. Blue-green / canary frontend
Reasoning Route percentage traffic to new static deploy — Sentry error rate gate.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Route percentage traffic to new static deploy — Sentry error rate gate.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Feature flag + load balancer weight
Trade-off Asset version mismatch if HTML from green loads JS from blue.

Atomic deploy HTML+JS together.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Blue-green / canary frontend.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
22. OpenTelemetry browser RUM
Reasoning Trace fetch, long tasks, route changes — correlate with backend trace IDs.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Trace fetch, long tasks, route changes — correlate with backend trace IDs.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
traceparent header on API calls from FE SDK
Trade-off Privacy — sample rates, PII scrubbing.

Staff-level observability.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: OpenTelemetry browser RUM.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.

23. XSS defense layers
Reasoning CSP, sanitize HTML, escape by default in framework, HttpOnly cookies.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

CSP, sanitize HTML, escape by default in framework, HttpOnly cookies.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
CSP: script-src 'self' 'nonce-{random}'
Trade-off DOMPurify config must forbid script tags.

Defense in depth answer expected.

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: XSS defense layers.

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.

24. CSRF defense layers
Reasoning SameSite cookies + CSRF token header + Origin check server-side.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

SameSite cookies + CSRF token header + Origin check server-side.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
SameSite=Lax default good; Strict for high security
Trade-off JWT in header not cookie avoids CSRF but XSS worse.

BFF cookie pattern.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: CSRF defense layers.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.

index.ts re-export all — bundler may import entire module tree.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
import { Button } from '@org/ui/Button' // direct path
Trade-off Barrels OK in published package entry only.

knip finds barrel perf issues.

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Barrel file anti-pattern.

33. GraphQL vs REST frontend
Reasoning GraphQL: client normalized cache Apollo/Relay, flexible queries. REST: simpler caching HTTP semantics + TanStack Query.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. GraphQL: client normalized cache Apollo/Relay, flexible queries.
  2. REST: simpler caching HTTP semantics + TanStack Query.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
Query colocation vs multiple REST endpoints
Trade-off GraphQL N+1 server problem not FE but mention.

Pick based on API team capabilities.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: GraphQL vs REST frontend.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.

34. tRPC / OpenAPI codegen
Reasoning End-to-end types — frontend knows backend contract at compile time.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

End-to-end types — frontend knows backend contract at compile time.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
openapi-typescript + zod validation
Trade-off Codegen drift if CI doesn't regenerate on spec change.

Contract-first API design.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile web BFF aggregates cart + offers + user in one round trip for checkout.

Why they chose this approach: GraphQL or REST BFF tailored to UI screens.

  • Pros: Fewer client round trips on 4G
  • Pros: Hide microservice sprawl
  • Cons: BFF becomes god service
  • Cons: Duplication across iOS/Android/web BFFs

35. Edge middleware auth
Reasoning Verify JWT/session at edge before origin — redirect unauthenticated early.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Verify JWT/session at edge before origin — redirect unauthenticated early.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Vercel middleware matcher /dashboard/*
Trade-off Edge limits Node APIs — use Web Crypto.

Reduces wasted SSR work.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Category PLP ISR revalidate 60s — sale hour drops to on-demand revalidate.

Why they chose this approach: Edge SSR for SEO PLP; CSR for account settings.

  • Pros: Google indexes sale categories
  • Pros: CDN serves stale while revalidating
  • Cons: Cache invalidation complexity on price drop
  • Cons: SSR cost at peak traffic

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
if (res.status === 429) await sleep(Retry-After header)
Trade-off Thundering herd if all clients retry same second — jitter.

Respect API contract.

  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
40. Staff interview narrative structure
Reasoning Context → options considered → decision → trade-offs accepted → measurable outcome.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Context → options considered → decision → trade-offs accepted → measurable outcome.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
"We chose pnpm; install -60%; cost: 2w migration"
Trade-off Quantify when possible.

Practice 2min stories aloud.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Staff interview narrative structure.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Trade-off accepted? State what you sacrificed (time-to-market, consistency, bundle size) and why the requirement forced it.
  • Defer to v2? Name one feature explicitly out of scope — offline, i18n, admin — to show prioritization discipline.
  • Proof metrics? Error rate, p95 latency, deploy frequency, CWV p75, support ticket volume — tie arch to observable outcomes.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

React Context API (22 questions) High priority

1. When should you use Context vs props vs external store?
  • Props: default — explicit data flow, easy to trace.
  • Context: deeply shared stable data (theme, auth, locale) consumed by many branches.
  • Store (Redux/Zustand): frequent updates, complex mutations, middleware, DevTools.

Context is not a performance optimization — it is a dependency injection mechanism.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. Why does a Context provider cause all consumers to re-render?
Reasoning React compares context value by reference. New object every render → all useContext subscribers scheduled for re-render, even if they only read theme and you changed unrelated cart. This is the #1 Context performance footgun.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. React compares context value by reference.
  2. New object every render → all useContext subscribers scheduled for re-render, even if they only read theme and you changed unrelated cart.
  3. This is the #1 Context performance footgun.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Bad vs good 
// BAD — new object every Parent render
function AppProvider({ children }) {
  const [user, setUser] = useState(null);
  return (
    <AppContext.Provider value={{ user, login: () => {}, logout: () => {} }}>
      {children}
    </AppContext.Provider>
  );
}

// GOOD — stable reference until deps change
const login = useCallback(async (u, p) => { ... }, []);
const logout = useCallback(() => { ... }, []);
const value = useMemo(() => ({ user, login, logout }), [user, login, logout]);
Fix hierarchy 1) memoize value 2) split contexts 3) selectors (use-context-selector / Zustand) 4) move fast-changing data to TanStack Query or external store.
Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. Split context pattern — state vs dispatch.
const StateCtx = createContext(null);
const DispatchCtx = createContext(null);

function Provider({ children }) {
  const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, init);
  return (
    <DispatchCtx.Provider value={dispatch}>
      <StateCtx.Provider value={state}>{children}</StateCtx.Provider>
    </DispatchCtx.Provider>
  );
}
// Components that only dispatch never re-render on state change
Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. useContextSelector pattern without external library.
function useAppSelector(selector) {
  const state = useContext(StateCtx);
  const selected = selector(state);
  const ref = useRef(selected);
  if (!Object.is(ref.current, selected)) ref.current = selected;
  return ref.current;
}

For production, prefer use-context-selector or Zustand with selectors — hand-rolled equality is error-prone.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. Context + useReducer vs Redux for FleetPanda-style apps.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Context+reducer fits moderate shared state, single team, no time-travel needs.
  2. Redux when: many slices, RTK Query, listener middleware, large team conventions.
  3. FleetPanda uses Context because assignment scope is one SPA with versioned localStorage.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Context+reducer fits moderate shared state, single team, no time-travel needs. Redux when: many slices, RTK Query, listener middleware, large team conventions. FleetPanda uses Context because assignment scope is one SPA with versioned localStorage.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
6. How to test components that use useContext?
const mock = createMockAppContext({ user: { role: 'admin', ... } });
render(
  <AppContext.Provider value={mock}>
    <ProtectedRoute allowedRoles={['admin']}><Dashboard /></ProtectedRoute>
  </AppContext.Provider>
);

Mock only what the test needs; spy on dispatch methods with vi.fn().

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
7. Nested providers — ordering and pitfalls.
<ThemeProvider>
  <AuthProvider>
    <QueryClientProvider>
      <ToastProvider>{children}</ToastProvider>
    </QueryClientProvider>
  </AuthProvider>
</ThemeProvider>

Inner hooks can use outer contexts. Avoid circular dependencies between providers. Toast should be high enough to cover all routes.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
8. Default context value — why undefined + custom hook throw?
const Ctx = createContext(undefined);
export function useCtx() {
  const v = useContext(Ctx);
  if (v === undefined) throw new Error('useCtx must be used within Provider');
  return v;
}

Catches missing provider at dev time instead of silent null bugs.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.

9. Context vs React 19 use() hook.
// use() can read context conditionally (hooks rules exception)
function Row({ showMeta }) {
  if (showMeta) {
    const theme = use(ThemeContext); // conditional read
  }
}

use() also unwraps promises in Suspense boundaries — different use case from classic context.

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.

10. Passing unstable functions through context.
const login = useCallback(async (u, p) => { ... }, []);
const logout = useCallback(() => { ... }, []);
// Stable fns prevent unnecessary value invalidation

Exception: if callback closes over changing state, include deps or use functional dispatch.

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

11. Multiple contexts vs one mega context
Reasoning Split by update frequency: ThemeContext (rare) vs CartContext (often). Mega context couples unrelated re-renders.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Split by update frequency: ThemeContext (rare) vs CartContext (often).
  2. Mega context couples unrelated re-renders.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
ThemeProvider + CartProvider separate
Trade-off Provider nesting depth — acceptable vs perf.

FleetPanda could split auth vs AppData at scale.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
12. Context for form state
Reasoning Large forms in context cause wide re-renders on every keystroke — prefer RHF local state or colocated state.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Large forms in context cause wide re-renders on every keystroke — prefer RHF local state or colocated state.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Wizard step context OK if only 3 fields shared across steps
Trade-off Context for every input field = perf smell.

Use context for submit handler registration not field values.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction

  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors

13. Provider at route layout level
Reasoning Scope admin state to /admin layout — unmount clears state automatically on navigate away.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Scope admin state to /admin layout — unmount clears state automatically on navigate away.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<Route path="/admin" element={<AdminProvider><AdminLayout /></AdminProvider>} />
Trade-off Remount provider resets state — intended or bug?

Persist to URL or server if state must survive route exit.

14. SSR context per request
Reasoning Server must create new store/context per HTTP request — never singleton on server module scope.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Server must create new store/context per HTTP request — never singleton on server module scope.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
export function createRequestContext() { return { user: null, data: {} }; }
Trade-off Global singleton leaks user A data to user B.

Critical SSR security interview topic.

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction

  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split

15. Hydration context mismatch
Reasoning Server context value must equal client initial render — else hydration error.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Server context value must equal client initial render — else hydration error.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Pass same preloadedState from server HTML script to client provider
Trade-off Client-only fetch in provider effect OK after hydrate.

Match FleetPanda localStorage hydrate pattern carefully with SSR.

16. createContext default value semantics
Reasoning Default used only when no Provider ancestor — usually undefined + throw hook pattern.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Default used only when no Provider ancestor — usually undefined + throw hook pattern.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
createContext(undefined)
Trade-off Using default as "real" value hides missing provider.

Document required provider in README.

17. forwardRef + context together
Reasoning Ref attaches DOM node; context supplies data — compose independently.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Ref attaches DOM node; context supplies data — compose independently.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
<ThemeContext.Provider><Input ref={ref} /></ThemeContext.Provider>
Trade-off React 19 ref as prop simplifies leaf components.

Don't put ref in context unless ref forwarding library.

18. Measuring context performance
Reasoning React Profiler record interaction; count consumers re-rendering; why-did-you-render dev tool.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

React Profiler record interaction; count consumers re-rendering; why-did-you-render dev tool.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Split context reduced renders from 40 components to 3 on cart update
Trade-off Optimize after measure — not preemptively.

Present numbers in senior interviews.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
19. Colocate provider closest to consumers
Reasoning Not every app needs root AppProvider — nested providers reduce re-render scope.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Not every app needs root AppProvider — nested providers reduce re-render scope.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
ModalProvider only wrapping modal subtree if possible
Trade-off Multiple providers at root still OK if memoized.

Architecture review question.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
20. Prop drilling threshold
Reasoning 2 levels: props fine. 3+ through unrelated components: consider composition or context.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. 2 levels: props fine.
  2. 3+ through unrelated components: consider composition or context.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
Children prop pattern passes rendered subtree
Trade-off Context for theme/auth/locale common exceptions at root.

Don't context for data one branch needs.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Split context when? When unrelated consumers re-render together — split by theme vs auth vs cart; or move fast-changing data to external store.
  • Debug re-renders? React DevTools highlight updates, why-did-you-render, or split context and compare commit counts.
  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
21. Immutable context updates
Reasoning Never mutate context state object in place — new reference triggers correct update detection.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Never mutate context state object in place — new reference triggers correct update detection.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
setState(prev => ({ ...prev, user: newUser }))
Trade-off Immer in provider reducer OK if produces new root reference.

Same rules as Redux reducers.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Measurable render reduction
  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split

  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

22. Migration off mega context
Reasoning Strangler: extract slice to Zustand/Query, dual-write both, migrate consumers file by file, remove from context.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Strangler: extract slice to Zustand/Query, dual-write both, migrate consumers file by file, remove from context.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Phase 1: server state to TanStack Query from AppContext
Trade-off Big-bang rewrite risky.

Tell migration story in staff interviews.

Context: ThemeContext + CartContext split — cart qty no longer re-renders entire PLP tree.

Why they chose this approach: Memoized value objects + split providers.

  • Pros: Simple mental model vs Redux for cart only
  • Cons: Still coarser than Zustand selectors
  • Cons: Provider hell if over-split

  • vs Zustand? Zustand selectors subscribe to slices; Context needs memoized value + split providers to match that granularity.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

Browser Internals (32 questions) High priority

1. Explain the browser rendering pipeline end-to-end.
Reasoning After bytes arrive, rendering is: parse HTML → build DOM; parse CSS → CSSOM; combine into render tree (visible nodes only); layout (compute geometry); paint (rasterize); composite (GPU layers). JS can trigger additional layout/paint cycles. This connects directly to the URL-bar journey in E2E section.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. After bytes arrive, rendering is: parse HTML → build DOM; parse CSS → CSSOM; combine into render tree (visible nodes only); layout (compute geometry); paint (rasterize); composite (GPU layers).
  2. JS can trigger additional layout/paint cycles.
  3. This connects directly to the URL-bar journey in E2E section.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
HTML bytes → Tokenizer → DOM tree
CSS bytes → CSSOM
DOM + CSSOM → Render tree → Layout → Paint → Composite
JS execution can mutate DOM → repeat Layout/Paint if needed
Trade-off Composite-only properties (transform, opacity) skip layout/paint when possible — key INP optimization.

Draw this pipeline on whiteboard when asked "what happens when page loads".

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Explain the browser rendering pipeline end-to-end..

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • DevTools? Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
  • Core Web Vital? Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
  • Safari / WebView? ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. Reflow vs repaint vs composite — what triggers each?
  • Reflow: geometry change — width, font, DOM insert/remove.
  • Repaint: visual only — color, visibility (no layout).
  • Composite: transform, opacity — often GPU, skips layout/paint.
/* Prefer compositor-friendly */
.animate { transform: translateX(100px); opacity: 0.5; }
/* Avoid */ .bad { left: 100px; top: 50px; }
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Reflow vs repaint vs composite — what triggers each?.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • DevTools? Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
  • Core Web Vital? Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
  • Safari / WebView? ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. Event loop — microtasks vs macrotasks (with full trace).
Reasoning JS is single-threaded. The event loop drains the call stack, then runs all microtasks (promises, queueMicrotask), then one macrotask (setTimeout, I/O, UI event), then renders if needed. Understanding order explains "why setState in promise runs before setTimeout".
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. JS is single-threaded.
  2. The event loop drains the call stack, then runs all microtasks (promises, queueMicrotask), then one macrotask (setTimeout, I/O, UI event), then renders if needed.
  3. Understanding order explains "why setState in promise runs before setTimeout".

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Classic interview trace 
console.log('1');
setTimeout(() => console.log('2'), 0);
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('3'));
queueMicrotask(() => console.log('4'));
console.log('5');
// Output: 1, 5, 3, 4, 2

Real bug: Measuring DOM in setTimeout(0) after React setState may run before React commit — use useLayoutEffect or flushSync when you must read post-update DOM.

INP connection Long microtask chains block paint — break work with scheduler/yield or workers for >50ms tasks.
Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Event loop — microtasks vs macrotasks (with full trace)..

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • DevTools? Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
  • Core Web Vital? Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
  • Safari / WebView? ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. How many threads/processes in modern Chrome for a tab?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Site-isolated renderer process per origin (generally).
  2. GPU process, network service, browser process separate.
  3. Multiple tabs same site may share process (with exceptions).
  4. Explains memory vs security trade-off.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Site-isolated renderer process per origin (generally). GPU process, network service, browser process separate. Multiple tabs same site may share process (with exceptions). Explains memory vs security trade-off.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: How many threads/processes in modern Chrome for a tab?.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • DevTools? Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
  • Core Web Vital? Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
  • Safari / WebView? ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. Same-origin policy and CORS — browser perspective.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Browser blocks reading cross-origin response unless server sends CORS headers.
  2. Simple GET may skip preflight; JSON POST with custom headers triggers OPTIONS preflight.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Browser blocks reading cross-origin response unless server sends CORS headers. Simple GET may skip preflight; JSON POST with custom headers triggers OPTIONS preflight.

Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Checkout BFF same-origin — browser never hits payment microservice CORS directly.

Why they chose this approach: BFF pattern for multi-backend ecommerce.

  • Pros: Single CORS surface
  • Pros: Hide internal service URLs
  • Cons: BFF team bottleneck
  • Cons: Extra latency hop
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • DevTools? Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
  • Core Web Vital? Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
  • Safari / WebView? ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
6. Cookie attributes — Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite.
Set-Cookie: session=abc; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Path=/; Max-Age=3600
  • HttpOnly: no document.cookie access — XSS mitigation
  • SameSite=Strict/Lax: CSRF mitigation
  • Secure: HTTPS only

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Cookie attributes — Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite..

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized

  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks

7. localStorage vs sessionStorage vs IndexedDB vs cookies.
StoreSizeSent to serverAPI
Cookie~4KBYesSync
localStorage~5MBNoSync — blocks main thread
IndexedDBLargeNoAsync

8. Service Worker lifecycle and caching strategies.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Install → wait → activate → fetch intercept.
  2. Strategies:

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Install → wait → activate → fetch intercept. Strategies:

  • Cache-first: static assets
  • Network-first: API data
  • Stale-while-revalidate: fonts, avatars
self.addEventListener('fetch', (e) => {
  e.respondWith(caches.match(e.request).then(c => c || fetch(e.request)));
});
Production example — Myntra

Context: PWA SW caches app shell; product API network-first during End of Reason Sale.

Why they chose this approach: Versioned precache + runtime image cache with TTL.

  • Pros: Repeat visit TTI faster
  • Pros: Offline recently viewed PLP
  • Cons: Stale price if cache-first on API
  • Cons: SW update UX needed
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • DevTools? Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
  • Core Web Vital? Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
  • Safari / WebView? ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
9. HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 for frontend perf.
  • H1: 6 conn limit per domain — bundling mattered
  • H2: multiplexing — smaller chunks OK
  • H3: QUIC over UDP — faster on lossy networks

Still minimize JS; protocol doesn't fix large bundles.

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 for frontend perf..

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player

10. Memory leaks in SPAs — common causes.
  • Intervals/listeners without cleanup
  • Detached DOM nodes held in closures
  • Global caches growing unbounded
  • WebSocket subscriptions
  • Third-party widgets
useEffect(() => {
  const handler = () => {};
  window.addEventListener('resize', handler);
  return () => window.removeEventListener('resize', handler);
}, []);
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Memory leaks in SPAs — common causes..

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
