11. requestAnimationFrame
Reasoning Schedule callback before next repaint ~60Hz — smooth animations synced to display.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Schedule callback before next repaint ~60Hz — smooth animations synced to display.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
function tick() { update(); requestAnimationFrame(tick); }
Trade-off Better than setInterval for visual updates.

Cancel with cancelAnimationFrame on unmount.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: requestAnimationFrame.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

12. requestIdleCallback
Reasoning Run non-critical work when browser idle — analytics batch low priority.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Run non-critical work when browser idle — analytics batch low priority.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
requestIdleCallback(() => sendAnalytics(), { timeout: 2000 })
Trade-off Safari limited — setTimeout fallback.

Don't starve if timeout fires.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: requestIdleCallback.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

Reasoning performance.getEntriesByType("navigation") — TTFB, DOMContentLoaded, loadEventEnd.

performance.getEntriesByType("navigation") — TTFB, DOMContentLoaded, loadEventEnd.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const nav = performance.getEntriesByType("navigation")[0]; nav.responseStart - nav.requestStart
Trade-off Legacy performance.timing deprecated.

Maps to URL bar journey phases.

14. PerformanceObserver
Reasoning Subscribe to LCP, layout-shift, longtask entries — RUM collection.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Subscribe to LCP, layout-shift, longtask entries — RUM collection.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
new PerformanceObserver(list => report(list.getEntries())).observe({ type: "largest-contentful-paint" })
Trade-off Buffer size limits — handle drops.

web-vitals library wraps this.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: PerformanceObserver.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • DevTools? Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
  • Core Web Vital? Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
  • Safari / WebView? ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
15. Long tasks >50ms
Reasoning Block main thread — delay input response hurting INP.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Block main thread — delay input response hurting INP.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
PerformanceObserver longtask entries
Trade-off Split task: scheduler.postTask or chunk loop.

Chrome DevTools Performance flame chart.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Long tasks >50ms.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • DevTools? Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
  • Core Web Vital? Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
  • Safari / WebView? ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
16. Web Workers
Reasoning Parallel JS thread — no DOM access — parse CSV, crypto, heavy compute.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Parallel JS thread — no DOM access — parse CSV, crypto, heavy compute.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const w = new Worker(new URL("./worker.ts", import.meta.url));
Trade-off Structured clone message cost — Transferable objects for buffers.

Offload from hot path.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Web Workers.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • DevTools? Name exact panel: Network waterfall, Performance trace, Application cache, Rendering paint flashing.
  • Core Web Vital? Map answer to LCP (load), INP (input delay), CLS (layout shift) — pick the most relevant one.
  • Safari / WebView? ITP cookie limits, missing APIs (e.g. some SW features), passive touch listeners — state what you polyfill or degrade.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
17. SharedWorker
Reasoning Shared between tabs same origin — rare vs BroadcastChannel.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Shared between tabs same origin — rare vs BroadcastChannel.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const sw = new SharedWorker("shared.js");
Trade-off Limited browser support niche.

Usually BroadcastChannel simpler.

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: SharedWorker.

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player

18. BroadcastChannel
Reasoning Same-origin tab messaging — logout sync cart sync.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Same-origin tab messaging — logout sync cart sync.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
new BroadcastChannel("auth").postMessage({ type: "LOGOUT" })
Trade-off Not cross-origin — use postMessage.

MFE same-origin tabs.

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: BroadcastChannel.

19. Page Visibility API
Reasoning document.hidden pause video polling timers save battery.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

document.hidden pause video polling timers save battery.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
document.addEventListener("visibilitychange", () => pauseIfHidden());
Trade-off Mobile background tab throttling aggressive.

FleetPanda map polling should pause.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Page Visibility API.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic

20. bfcache back-forward cache
Reasoning Page snapshot in memory — instant back navigation.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Page snapshot in memory — instant back navigation.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
pageshow event persisted property
Trade-off unload listeners disable bfcache — use pagehide.

See journeys section Q9.

Production example — Myntra

Context: PWA SW caches app shell; product API network-first during End of Reason Sale.

Why they chose this approach: Versioned precache + runtime image cache with TTL.

  • Pros: Repeat visit TTI faster
  • Pros: Offline recently viewed PLP
  • Cons: Stale price if cache-first on API
  • Cons: SW update UX needed

beforeunload unreliable especially mobile — don't depend for save.

pagehide + sendBeacon for analytics

Autosave debounced better UX.

Efficient visible detection — lazy load images infinite scroll.

Better than window resize for component.

27. Mutation Observer
Reasoning DOM tree changes — legacy widgets tests.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

DOM tree changes — legacy widgets tests.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
new MutationObserver(mutations => sync()).observe(el, { childList: true })
Trade-off Expensive on large subtrees — avoid hot paths.

React rarely needs — use refs/effects.

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Mutation Observer.

DOM & Events (28 questions) High priority

1. DOM tree vs shadow DOM vs virtual DOM.
  • DOM: browser's live tree of nodes.
  • Shadow DOM: encapsulated subtree — Web Components.
  • Virtual DOM: React's JS representation — diffed then patched to real DOM.
Production example — Instagram

Context: Feed uses virtualized list + IO sentinel for infinite scroll — constant DOM node count.

Why they chose this approach: react-window + Intersection Observer fetchNextPage.

  • Pros: 10k posts scrollable without crash
  • Pros: Prefetch next page before sentinel visible
  • Cons: Focus management complex in virtual list
  • Cons: Dynamic height rows hard to measure
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. Event propagation — capture, target, bubble.
parent.addEventListener('click', handler, true);  // capture phase
child.addEventListener('click', handler);         // bubble phase (default)
// Order: capture (root→target) → target → bubble (target→root)

event.stopPropagation() stops further propagation; stopImmediatePropagation() blocks other listeners on same node.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Event propagation — capture, target, bubble..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. Event delegation pattern.
document.querySelector('#list').addEventListener('click', (e) => {
  const row = e.target.closest('[data-id]');
  if (!row) return;
  handleRowClick(row.dataset.id);
});

One listener for dynamic lists — React uses delegation at root for synthetic events (legacy model).

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Event delegation pattern..

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. Passive event listeners — scroll performance.
element.addEventListener('touchstart', handler, { passive: true });
// Browser won't wait for preventDefault — smoother scroll

Non-passive touch/wheel listeners can block scrolling — Lighthouse flags this.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Feed uses virtualized list + IO sentinel for infinite scroll — constant DOM node count.

Why they chose this approach: react-window + Intersection Observer fetchNextPage.

  • Pros: 10k posts scrollable without crash
  • Pros: Prefetch next page before sentinel visible
  • Cons: Focus management complex in virtual list
  • Cons: Dynamic height rows hard to measure
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. document.querySelector vs getElementById vs matches/closest.
const btn = document.getElementById('save');           // fastest id lookup
const row = e.target.closest('tr[data-order-id]');   // event target traversal
const isCard = el.matches('.card');                  // boolean test
Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: document.querySelector vs getElementById vs matches/closest..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
6. DOM manipulation performance — DocumentFragment.
const frag = document.createDocumentFragment();
items.forEach(item => frag.appendChild(createRow(item)));
tbody.appendChild(frag); // single reflow

Batch DOM writes; avoid interleaved read/write (layout thrashing).

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: DOM manipulation performance — DocumentFragment..

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
7. Layout thrashing — read/write interleaving.
// BAD — forces sync layout each iteration
els.forEach(el => { el.style.left = el.offsetWidth + 'px'; });
// GOOD — read all, then write all
const widths = els.map(el => el.offsetWidth);
els.forEach((el, i) => { el.style.left = widths[i] + 'px'; });
Production example — X (Twitter)

Context: Realtime timeline — WebSocket push, optimistic compose, virtualized list for high event rate. Relevant when discussing: Layout thrashing — read/write interleaving..

Why they chose this approach: Batch DOM updates during viral events; reconnect with dedupe ids.

  • Pros: Sub-second timeline updates
  • Pros: Optimistic tweet post UX
  • Cons: WS fragile on corporate networks
  • Cons: Main-thread pressure during live events
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
8. CustomEvent for decoupled DOM communication.
window.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('cart:updated', { detail: { count: 3 } }));
window.addEventListener('cart:updated', (e) => updateBadge(e.detail.count));

Useful for vanilla widgets; micro-frontends often use same pattern on shared bus.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: CustomEvent for decoupled DOM communication..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
9. Imperative DOM in React — when acceptable?
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Third-party libs (Leaflet, D3, CodeMirror), focus management, measuring.
  2. Pattern: ref + useEffect mount/update/cleanup.
  3. FleetPanda's LeafletMapView is the canonical example.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Third-party libs (Leaflet, D3, CodeMirror), focus management, measuring. Pattern: ref + useEffect mount/update/cleanup. FleetPanda's LeafletMapView is the canonical example.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: Imperative DOM in React — when acceptable?.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
10. Accessibility tree vs DOM tree.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Browser builds accessibility tree from DOM + ARIA — what screen readers use.
  2. Hidden CSS ( display:none ) removes from a11y tree; aria-hidden explicitly hides decorative elements.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Browser builds accessibility tree from DOM + ARIA — what screen readers use. Hidden CSS (display:none) removes from a11y tree; aria-hidden explicitly hides decorative elements.

Production example — X (Twitter)

Context: Realtime timeline — WebSocket push, optimistic compose, virtualized list for high event rate. Relevant when discussing: Accessibility tree vs DOM tree..

Why they chose this approach: Batch DOM updates during viral events; reconnect with dedupe ids.

  • Pros: Sub-second timeline updates
  • Pros: Optimistic tweet post UX
  • Cons: WS fragile on corporate networks
  • Cons: Main-thread pressure during live events
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.

  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
11. innerHTML vs textContent
Reasoning innerHTML parses HTML — XSS if user controlled. textContent escapes to text node.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. innerHTML parses HTML — XSS if user controlled.
  2. textContent escapes to text node.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
el.textContent = userInput
Trade-off innerHTML only after DOMPurify.sanitize.

Security fundamental.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: innerHTML vs textContent.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
12. insertAdjacentHTML
Reasoning Parse HTML string at position beforebegin afterbegin beforeend afterend.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Parse HTML string at position beforebegin afterbegin beforeend afterend.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
el.insertAdjacentHTML("beforeend", "<li>item</li>")
Trade-off Same XSS rules as innerHTML.

Efficient template insertion.

Reasoning Bitmask relationship between nodes — contains precedes follows.

Bitmask relationship between nodes — contains precedes follows.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
node.compareDocumentPosition(other) & Node.DOCUMENT_POSITION_FOLLOWING

15. getBoundingClientRect
Reasoning Viewport-relative box including scroll transform — tooltip positioning.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Viewport-relative box including scroll transform — tooltip positioning.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const rect = el.getBoundingClientRect(); top = rect.bottom + 8
Trade-off Subpixel values — round carefully.

Use with fixed position popovers.

16. scrollIntoView
Reasoning Scroll ancestor to show element — focus management SPA routes.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Scroll ancestor to show element — focus management SPA routes.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
el.scrollIntoView({ behavior: "smooth", block: "nearest" })
Trade-off Can scroll entire page jarringly — block option.

Hash link navigation native behavior.

19. Pointer events unified input
Reasoning pointerdown covers mouse touch pen — pointer-events none disables hit.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

pointerdown covers mouse touch pen — pointer-events none disables hit.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
@media (pointer: coarse) { larger hit targets }
Trade-off Touch delay removed with pointer events.

Mobile desktop unified handlers.

Auto-remove after first invoke — { once: true }.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
el.addEventListener("click", handler, { once: true })
Trade-off Cleaner than removeListener inside handler.

One-shot analytics.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
21. AbortSignal in addEventListener
Reasoning signal abort removes listener — tie to AbortController lifecycle.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

signal abort removes listener — tie to AbortController lifecycle.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
el.addEventListener("click", fn, { signal: ac.signal })
Trade-off Same controller aborts fetch + listeners.

Modern cleanup pattern.

Production example — Instagram

Context: Infinite feed, media-heavy, scroll perf — virtualize and defer video autoplay to viewport. Relevant when discussing: AbortSignal in addEventListener.

Why they chose this approach: Cursor pagination + reserved media aspect ratio + external store for likes.

  • Pros: Feed scroll 60fps target
  • Pros: Strong media CDN pipeline
  • Cons: Hydration cost on web
  • Cons: Moderation + ads complicate feed logic
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • React vs DOM? React batches updates via Fiber, synthetic events, declarative diff — contrast with manual reflow from direct DOM writes.
  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
22. input vs change events
Reasoning input fires every keystroke — change on commit blur select.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

input fires every keystroke — change on commit blur select.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
search on input debounced; validate on change
Trade-off React controlled inputs use onChange for input event.

Search UX uses input.

Production example — X (Twitter)

Context: Realtime timeline — WebSocket push, optimistic compose, virtualized list for high event rate. Relevant when discussing: input vs change events.

Why they chose this approach: Batch DOM updates during viral events; reconnect with dedupe ids.

  • Pros: Sub-second timeline updates
  • Pros: Optimistic tweet post UX
  • Cons: WS fragile on corporate networks
  • Cons: Main-thread pressure during live events

  • A11y? Semantic elements, ARIA only when needed, focus management on route/modal — tie DOM API to screen reader behavior.
  • Low-end perf? Layout thrashing from read/write interleave, large DOM trees — mention virtualization or content-visibility.

compositionstart/update/end for CJK input — don't search mid-composition.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
if (e.isComposing) return; in keydown handler
Trade-off Breaks search for Japanese users if ignored.

Internationalization detail seniors know.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Composition events IME.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

28. Don't fight React with DOM
Reasoning Direct DOM mutation breaks React virtual DOM — use refs effects portals.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Direct DOM mutation breaks React virtual DOM — use refs effects portals.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Leaflet imperative map in useEffect not document.getElementById in render
Trade-off Escape hatches only for integrations.

FleetPanda LeafletMapView pattern.

Production example — X (Twitter)

Context: Realtime timeline — WebSocket push, optimistic compose, virtualized list for high event rate. Relevant when discussing: Don't fight React with DOM.

JavaScript & TypeScript (30 questions) High priority

1. Event loop + async/await execution order.
async function foo() {
  console.log('A');
  await bar();
  console.log('B'); // microtask after bar resolves
}
async function bar() { console.log('C'); }
foo(); console.log('D');
// A, C, D, B
Production example — X (Twitter)

Context: Timeline merge batches incoming tweet promises — microtask queue floods if unbounded.

Why they chose this approach: requestAnimationFrame batch DOM inserts for 50+ new tweets/sec during live event.

  • Pros: Main thread stays responsive
  • Pros: No lost tweets on race
  • Cons: Complex batching logic
  • Cons: Debugging async order harder
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • TypeScript boundary? Narrow types at API parse (Zod/io-ts), avoid any on event payloads, discriminated unions for state machines.
  • Junior bug? Pick classic: closure in loop, mutable default args, floating promise without catch, == vs === on IDs.
  • Testing? Unit for pure logic, RTL for DOM behavior, MSW for network — match test type to failure mode.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. Closures — practical use and memory implications.
function createCounter() {
  let count = 0;
  return () => ++count; // closes over count
}

Leaks when closure captures large scope unnecessarily — null out refs when done.

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Closures — practical use and memory implications..

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • TypeScript boundary? Narrow types at API parse (Zod/io-ts), avoid any on event payloads, discriminated unions for state machines.
  • Junior bug? Pick classic: closure in loop, mutable default args, floating promise without catch, == vs === on IDs.
  • Testing? Unit for pure logic, RTL for DOM behavior, MSW for network — match test type to failure mode.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. Prototype chain vs class syntax.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. class Foo extends Bar is syntactic sugar over prototype delegation.
  2. Know Object.create , hasOwnProperty vs in-chain property lookup for interviews.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

4. TypeScript structural typing vs nominal.
interface User { id: string; name: string; }
interface Admin { id: string; name: string; role: 'admin'; }
function greet(u: User) { return u.name; }
greet(admin); // OK if shape compatible — structural

Branded types for nominal simulation: type UserId = string & { readonly brand: unique symbol }.

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: TypeScript structural typing vs nominal..

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns

  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain

5. Generics — constrain and infer.
function pick<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, keys: K[]): Pick<T, K> {
  const result = {} as Pick<T, K>;
  keys.forEach(k => { result[k] = obj[k]; });
  return result;
}
Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Generics — constrain and infer..

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player

  • Testing? Unit for pure logic, RTL for DOM behavior, MSW for network — match test type to failure mode.

6. Discriminated unions for API results.
type Result<T> =
  | { ok: true; data: T }
  | { ok: false; error: string };

function handle(r: Result<User>) {
  if (r.ok) console.log(r.data.name); // narrowed
  else console.error(r.error);
}

Context: Realtime timeline — WebSocket push, optimistic compose, virtualized list for high event rate. Relevant when discussing: Discriminated unions for API results..

7. Promise.all vs allSettled vs race.
  • all: fail fast on first rejection
  • allSettled: wait for all — partial success dashboards
  • race: first settle wins — timeout pattern
Production example — X (Twitter)

Context: Timeline merge batches incoming tweet promises — microtask queue floods if unbounded.

Why they chose this approach: requestAnimationFrame batch DOM inserts for 50+ new tweets/sec during live event.

  • Pros: Main thread stays responsive
  • Pros: No lost tweets on race
  • Cons: Complex batching logic
  • Cons: Debugging async order harder

  • Junior bug? Pick classic: closure in loop, mutable default args, floating promise without catch, == vs === on IDs.

8. Debounce vs throttle — implement debounce.
function debounce(fn, ms) {
  let t;
  return (...args) => {
    clearTimeout(t);
    t = setTimeout(() => fn(...args), ms);
  };
}
Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Debounce vs throttle — implement debounce..

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player

undefined absent; null intentional empty — use === distinguish.

API design clarity.

12. == vs ===
Reasoning Always === except null check (x == null catches undefined).
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Always === except null check (x == null catches undefined).

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
if (value == null) clear();
Trade-off NaN === NaN false — use Number.isNaN.

Lint eqeqeq rule.

13. Object.is
Reasoning SameValue equality — +0 !== -0, NaN equals NaN.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

SameValue equality — +0 !== -0, NaN equals NaN.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Object.is(NaN, NaN) // true
Trade-off Map keys use SameValue.

Rare direct use.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Object.is.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • TypeScript boundary? Narrow types at API parse (Zod/io-ts), avoid any on event payloads, discriminated unions for state machines.
  • Junior bug? Pick classic: closure in loop, mutable default args, floating promise without catch, == vs === on IDs.
  • Testing? Unit for pure logic, RTL for DOM behavior, MSW for network — match test type to failure mode.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
14. Optional chaining ?.
Reasoning Short-circuit undefined access — obj?.a?.b
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Short-circuit undefined access — obj?.a?.b

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
user?.profile?.email ?? "unknown"
Trade-off Not substitute for data modeling.

With nullish coalescing common pair.

Production example — Netflix

Context: Streaming UI prioritizes playback quality and browse LCP — perf budgets enforced per route. Relevant when discussing: Optional chaining ?..

Why they chose this approach: Measure CrUX p75 before shipping player or homepage changes.

  • Pros: Strong RUM culture
  • Pros: Edge CDN for static assets
  • Cons: Personalization complicates caching
  • Cons: DRM constraints on web player

15. Nullish coalescing ??
Reasoning Default only null/undefined not 0 or "".
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Default only null/undefined not 0 or "".

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
count ?? 0 // keeps 0; count || 0 replaces 0
Trade-off || treats 0 as falsy bug.

Config defaults pattern.

Production example — X (Twitter)

Context: Realtime timeline — WebSocket push, optimistic compose, virtualized list for high event rate. Relevant when discussing: Nullish coalescing ??.

Why they chose this approach: Batch DOM updates during viral events; reconnect with dedupe ids.

  • Pros: Sub-second timeline updates

  • Cons: WS fragile on corporate networks
  • Cons: Main-thread pressure during live events

16. Map vs Object records
Reasoning Map any keys object order insertion; frequent add delete.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Map any keys object order insertion; frequent add delete.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const cache = new Map(); cache.set(key, val)
Trade-off JSON.stringify Map poorly — not for API payload.

WeakMap for metadata private.

26. Conditional types
Reasoning T extends U ? X : Y — infer keyword extract types.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. T extends U ?
  2. X : Y — infer keyword extract types.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Example
type Return<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never
Trade-off Complex — read library types.

Advanced TS interviews.

30. Zod runtime validation
Reasoning Schema.parse at API boundary — don't trust JSON types.

Schema.parse at API boundary — don't trust JSON types.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
const User = z.object({ id: z.string(), email: z.string().email() }); User.parse(json)
Trade-off Compile time types separate from runtime — generate zod from openapi optional.

FleetPanda localStorage should use this.

CSS vs SCSS vs Tailwind (24 questions) High priority

1. Compare CSS, SCSS, and Tailwind — when to choose each?
ApproachProsCons
Plain CSSZero tooling, native vars, cascade layersScale needs discipline (BEM)
SCSSVariables, nesting, mixins, partialsBuild step; nesting abuse
TailwindSpeed, design tokens, purge unusedVerbose JSX, learning curve

Trend: CSS native features (nesting, vars) reduce SCSS need; Tailwind dominates greenfield startups.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.

  • Pros: Fast iteration on seller portal
  • Pros: Purge removes unused utilities in prod
  • Cons: HTML noise in consumer components
  • Cons: Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. SCSS nesting — best practices and pitfalls.
// GOOD — BEM with shallow nesting
.card {
  &__title { font-size: 1.25rem; }
  &--featured { border-color: blue; }
}
// BAD — mirrors DOM depth, specificity explosion
.page .sidebar .nav .item a { color: red; }
Production example — Amazon

Context: Legacy Seller Central SCSS partials migrating to CSS modules per route strangler.

Why they chose this approach: Co-locate module per component; token map from SCSS vars to CSS vars.

  • Pros: No global cascade bugs on new code
  • Pros: Incremental migration
  • Cons: Dual styling systems during transition
  • Cons: Developer confusion which pattern to use
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. SCSS mixins vs CSS @layer — modern alternative.
@mixin focus-ring { outline: 2px solid var(--focus); }
// CSS today:
@layer utilities {
  .focus-ring:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--focus); }
}

Mixins still useful for complex parameterized patterns; layers control cascade order natively.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Legacy Seller Central SCSS partials migrating to CSS modules per route strangler.

Why they chose this approach: Co-locate module per component; token map from SCSS vars to CSS vars.

  • Pros: No global cascade bugs on new code
  • Pros: Incremental migration
  • Cons: Dual styling systems during transition
  • Cons: Developer confusion which pattern to use
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. Tailwind @apply — when acceptable?
@layer components {
  .btn-primary {
    @apply inline-flex items-center rounded-md bg-blue-600 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-blue-700;
  }
}

OK for design-system primitives. Overuse duplicates Tailwind benefits — prefer utilities in markup for one-offs.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.

  • Pros: Fast iteration on seller portal
  • Pros: Purge removes unused utilities in prod
  • Cons: HTML noise in consumer components
  • Cons: Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. Tailwind JIT and purging — how bundle stays small.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Scans source files for class strings; generates only used utilities.
  2. Dynamic class names break purge:

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Scans source files for class strings; generates only used utilities. Dynamic class names break purge:

// BAD — purge can't see full string
const color = 'red'; className={`text-${color}-500`}
// GOOD
const map = { red: 'text-red-500', blue: 'text-blue-500' };
Production example — Myntra

Context: Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.

  • Pros: Fast iteration on seller portal
  • Pros: Purge removes unused utilities in prod
  • Cons: HTML noise in consumer components
  • Cons: Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
6. Design tokens — implement across CSS/SCSS/Tailwind.
/* CSS vars — source of truth */
:root { --space-4: 1rem; --color-brand: #2563eb; }
/* tailwind.config theme.extend */
colors: { brand: 'var(--color-brand)' }

Single token source synced to Tailwind theme and SCSS variables via Style Dictionary.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.

  • Pros: Fast iteration on seller portal
  • Pros: Purge removes unused utilities in prod
  • Cons: HTML noise in consumer components
  • Cons: Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing

7. CSS Modules vs Tailwind in React.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Modules: scoped class names, colocated Button.module.css , zero runtime.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Modules: scoped class names, colocated Button.module.css, zero runtime.

Tailwind: faster iteration, consistent spacing scale, worse for highly dynamic arbitrary styles.

Hybrid common: Tailwind layout + CSS modules for complex animations.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.

  • Pros: Fast iteration on seller portal
  • Pros: Purge removes unused utilities in prod
  • Cons: HTML noise in consumer components
  • Cons: Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing

8. SCSS @use vs deprecated @import.
@use 'tokens/colors' as c;
.button { background: c.$primary; }

@use loads once, namespaced — fixes duplicate CSS and global pollution from @import.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Legacy Seller Central SCSS partials migrating to CSS modules per route strangler.

Why they chose this approach: Co-locate module per component; token map from SCSS vars to CSS vars.

  • Pros: No global cascade bugs on new code
  • Pros: Incremental migration
  • Cons: Dual styling systems during transition
  • Cons: Developer confusion which pattern to use
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

// tailwind.config: darkMode: 'class'
<html class="dark">
<div class="bg-white dark:bg-slate-900">

Or media strategy uses prefers-color-scheme — class strategy allows user toggle.

10. CSS-in-JS vs Tailwind in 2025 — senior take.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. CSS-in-JS (styled-components) loses favor: runtime cost, RSC incompatibility, streaming issues.
  2. Tailwind + CSS modules + vanilla-extract (zero runtime) preferred for new apps.
  3. Know trade-offs, not tribalism.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

CSS-in-JS (styled-components) loses favor: runtime cost, RSC incompatibility, streaming issues. Tailwind + CSS modules + vanilla-extract (zero runtime) preferred for new apps. Know trade-offs, not tribalism.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.

  • Pros: Fast iteration on seller portal

  • Cons: HTML noise in consumer components
  • Cons: Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing

11. BEM naming convention
Reasoning Block__element--modifier avoids specificity wars plain CSS.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Block__element--modifier avoids specificity wars plain CSS.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
.card__title--featured
Trade-off Verbose class names — Tailwind avoids differently.

Legacy codebase common.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: BEM naming convention.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
12. ITCSS / SMACSS layers
Reasoning Ordering: settings tools generic elements objects components utilities.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Ordering: settings tools generic elements objects components utilities.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Utilities last win cascade intentionally
Trade-off Tailwind utilities = ITCSS utilities layer.

Architecture not just class names.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: ITCSS / SMACSS layers.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

13. PostCSS in all stacks
Reasoning Autoprefixer cssnano nesting — build step even "plain CSS".
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Autoprefixer cssnano nesting — build step even "plain CSS".

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
postcss.config.js in Vite webpack
Trade-off Native CSS nesting reduces need nesting plugin.

Know pipeline.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: PostCSS in all stacks.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
22. Native CSS nesting
Reasoning Chrome 120+ nest & parent selector — reduce Sass need.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Chrome 120+ nest & parent selector — reduce Sass need.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
.card { & __title { } }
Trade-off Tooling must pass through postcss preset env.

Sass still has mixins functions.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Native CSS nesting.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
23. Stylelint enforcement
Reasoning Ban unknown units, enforce property order, a11y contrast plugin.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Ban unknown units, enforce property order, a11y contrast plugin.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
stylelint-config-standard-scss
Trade-off CI stylelint on PR.

Design system governance.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Stylelint enforcement.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.

  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.

24. Interview answer CSS vs SCSS vs Tailwind
Reasoning Structure: team velocity bundle theming SSR constraints — pick with trade-offs not religion.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Structure: team velocity bundle theming SSR constraints — pick with trade-offs not religion.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
"Greenfield dashboard → Tailwind + CSS vars tokens; legacy enterprise SCSS modules gradual Tailwind"
Trade-off Quantify migration cost.

Staff decision framework.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Internal tools built with Tailwind + design tokens; consumer site hybrid CSS modules + tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Tailwind for speed on admin; tokens from Style Dictionary for brand consistency.

  • Pros: Fast iteration on seller portal
  • Pros: Purge removes unused utilities in prod
  • Cons: HTML noise in consumer components
  • Cons: Migration from legacy SCSS ongoing
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Greenfield 2025? Tailwind or CSS modules + tokens; avoid global SCSS soup unless team mandate; co-locate styles with components.
  • SCSS migration? Strangler: new components CSS modules, token map from $variables to custom properties, delete unused partials incrementally.
  • Bundle impact? Purge/Treeshake Tailwind; critical CSS for above-fold; measure computed stylesheet size in Coverage tab.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

Micro-frontend Communication (28 questions) High priority

1. Overview — how do micro-frontends share data across pages?

Patterns ranked by common usage:

  1. URL / query params — shareable, bookmarkable
  2. Custom events / event bus — loose coupling same page
  3. Shared state store — module federation shared singleton
  4. Backend as source of truth — API + cache (best for cross-domain)
  5. postMessage — cross-origin iframes
  6. BroadcastChannel / localStorage events — same-origin tabs
Production example — Amazon

Context: Retail shell loads checkout + recommendations remotes; shared QueryClient and design tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Module Federation + semver contract on shared deps.

  • Pros: Team autonomy per domain
  • Pros: Incremental deploy
  • Cons: Integration test matrix shell×remote versions
  • Cons: Version skew white screen incidents
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Version drift? Shared dependency semver contract, integration tests shell+remote, canary remote URL, fallback UI if remoteEntry fails.
  • Cross-team testing? Contract tests on exposed modules, e2e against staging shell with production remote pins, visual regression on shell layout.
  • Security? Remote code runs in user session — CSP, subresource integrity on remoteEntry, auth token not in shared global.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. Module Federation — share React and global store.
// webpack shell
new ModuleFederationPlugin({
  shared: {
    react: { singleton: true, requiredVersion: '^19', eager: true },
    'shared-store': { singleton: true, import: 'shared-store' }
  }
});
// remote MFE imports same singleton store instance

Version mismatch on shared deps = runtime hook errors — align in CI.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Retail shell loads checkout + recommendations remotes; shared QueryClient and design tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Module Federation + semver contract on shared deps.

  • Pros: Team autonomy per domain
  • Pros: Incremental deploy
  • Cons: Integration test matrix shell×remote versions
  • Cons: Version skew white screen incidents
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Version drift? Shared dependency semver contract, integration tests shell+remote, canary remote URL, fallback UI if remoteEntry fails.
  • Cross-team testing? Contract tests on exposed modules, e2e against staging shell with production remote pins, visual regression on shell layout.
  • Security? Remote code runs in user session — CSP, subresource integrity on remoteEntry, auth token not in shared global.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. Custom event bus for cross-MFE communication.
type BusEvent = { type: 'USER_LOGIN'; payload: { userId: string } };

class EventBus {
  #target = new EventTarget();
  emit(event: BusEvent) {
    this.#target.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent(event.type, { detail: event.payload }));
  }
  on<T extends BusEvent['type']>(type: T, handler: (p: ExtractPayload<T>) => void) {
    this.#target.addEventListener(type, (e) => handler(e.detail));
  }
}
export const bus = new EventBus(); // shared module via federation
Production example — Amazon

Context: Retail shell loads checkout + recommendations remotes; shared QueryClient and design tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Module Federation + semver contract on shared deps.

  • Pros: Team autonomy per domain
  • Pros: Incremental deploy
  • Cons: Integration test matrix shell×remote versions
  • Cons: Version skew white screen incidents
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Version drift? Shared dependency semver contract, integration tests shell+remote, canary remote URL, fallback UI if remoteEntry fails.
  • Cross-team testing? Contract tests on exposed modules, e2e against staging shell with production remote pins, visual regression on shell layout.
  • Security? Remote code runs in user session — CSP, subresource integrity on remoteEntry, auth token not in shared global.

4. Passing data when navigating shell → remote route.
// Shell router
navigate('/shop/checkout?cartId=abc123');
// Remote reads
const cartId = new URLSearchParams(location.search).get('cartId');
// Or React Router location.state (not bookmarkable)
navigate('/checkout', { state: { cartId } });

Senior rule: persist critical state in URL or server; location.state for ephemeral UI.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Retail shell loads checkout + recommendations remotes; shared QueryClient and design tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Module Federation + semver contract on shared deps.

  • Pros: Team autonomy per domain
  • Pros: Incremental deploy
  • Cons: Integration test matrix shell×remote versions
  • Cons: Version skew white screen incidents

  • Version drift? Shared dependency semver contract, integration tests shell+remote, canary remote URL, fallback UI if remoteEntry fails.
  • Cross-team testing? Contract tests on exposed modules, e2e against staging shell with production remote pins, visual regression on shell layout.

5. postMessage for iframe-based micro-frontends.
// Child iframe
window.parent.postMessage({ type: 'RESIZE', height: 800 }, 'https://shell.example.com');
// Shell
window.addEventListener('message', (e) => {
  if (e.origin !== 'https://shop.example.com') return; // ALWAYS validate origin
  if (e.data.type === 'RESIZE') iframe.style.height = e.data.height + 'px';
});
Production example — Amazon

Context: Retail shell loads checkout + recommendations remotes; shared QueryClient and design tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Module Federation + semver contract on shared deps.

  • Pros: Team autonomy per domain
  • Pros: Incremental deploy
  • Cons: Integration test matrix shell×remote versions
  • Cons: Version skew white screen incidents

6. BroadcastChannel across MFE tabs on same origin.
const channel = new BroadcastChannel('app-events');
channel.postMessage({ type: 'LOGOUT' });
channel.onmessage = (e) => { if (e.data.type === 'LOGOUT') clearSession(); };

All MFE bundles on same origin share channel — logout in one tab syncs all.

7. localStorage storage event for cross-tab sync.
// Tab A
localStorage.setItem('cart', JSON.stringify(cart));
// Tab B
window.addEventListener('storage', (e) => {
  if (e.key === 'cart') setCart(JSON.parse(e.newValue));
});

Only fires in other tabs, not the writer. Same-origin only.

8. Shared auth token across micro-frontends — secure pattern.
  • HttpOnly cookie on shared parent domain (.example.com)
  • Shell handles login; MFEs call API with credentials: 'include'
  • Never pass JWT in postMessage or query string
  • Short-lived access + silent refresh via shell BFF

9. Routing coordination — shell owns router vs federated routes.
// Shell routes
<Route path="/shop/*" element={<ShopRemote />} />
<Route path="/account/*" element={<AccountRemote />} />
// Remote owns sub-routes internally with basename="/shop"

Single browser history — shell must not fight remote for navigation events.

10. Cross-MFE CSS isolation — Shadow DOM vs CSS Modules vs prefix.
  • Shadow DOM: Strong isolation — hard with React portals/modals
  • CSS Modules / scoped: Practical default
  • Prefix convention: .shop-app__btn — cheap, discipline-heavy
  • Tailwind prefix: prefix: 'shop-' in remote config

<ErrorBoundary fallback={<ShopDown />}><ShopRemote /></ErrorBoundary>
Trade-off Error telemetry tag remote name.

Resilience requirement production MFE.

Production example — Amazon

Context: Retail shell loads checkout + recommendations remotes; shared QueryClient and design tokens.

Why they chose this approach: Module Federation + semver contract on shared deps.

  • Pros: Team autonomy per domain
  • Pros: Incremental deploy
  • Cons: Integration test matrix shell×remote versions
  • Cons: Version skew white screen incidents
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Version drift? Shared dependency semver contract, integration tests shell+remote, canary remote URL, fallback UI if remoteEntry fails.
  • Cross-team testing? Contract tests on exposed modules, e2e against staging shell with production remote pins, visual regression on shell layout.
  • Security? Remote code runs in user session — CSP, subresource integrity on remoteEntry, auth token not in shared global.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

import("shop/App") on route — not on initial shell load.
