High Priority Misc (25 questions)

1. XSS types and prevention in React apps.
  • Stored/reflected XSS: sanitize HTML, CSP
  • DOM XSS: never innerHTML with user input
  • React escapes JSX by default — danger: dangerouslySetInnerHTML
import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';
<div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: DOMPurify.sanitize(html) }} />
Production example — Myntra

Context: User review photos + HTML snippets sanitized with DOMPurify before dangerouslySetInnerHTML.

Why they chose this approach: Allowlist tags only; CSP script-src strict on seller portal.

  • Pros: Blocked script injection in reviews
  • Pros: Compliance with security audit
  • Cons: Rich text limited vs Word paste
  • Cons: CSP breaks inline third-party widgets
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
2. CSRF protection for cookie-based SPAs.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. SameSite=Lax/Strict cookies + CSRF token header on mutations + verify Origin/Referer server-side.
  2. Double-submit cookie pattern for legacy.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

SameSite=Lax/Strict cookies + CSRF token header on mutations + verify Origin/Referer server-side. Double-submit cookie pattern for legacy.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: CSRF protection for cookie-based SPAs..

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
3. OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow for public SPA clients.
// Generate code_verifier + code_challenge (S256)
// Redirect to auth server with challenge
// Exchange code + verifier for tokens — no client secret in browser
Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow for public SPA clients..

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
4. TanStack Query — staleTime vs gcTime (cacheTime).
  • staleTime: how long data considered fresh (no refetch on mount)
  • gcTime: how long inactive cache kept in memory
useQuery({ queryKey: ['user'], queryFn: fetchUser, staleTime: 5 * 60 * 1000 });
Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: TanStack Query — staleTime vs gcTime (cacheTime)..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
5. Web Vitals — INP debugging workflow.
  1. Find long tasks in Performance panel
  2. Split handlers — defer non-critical work
  3. Web worker for heavy compute
  4. Reduce re-renders on input
  5. Check third-party scripts
Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Web Vitals — INP debugging workflow..

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
6. Accessibility audit checklist for release.
  • Keyboard-only navigation all flows
  • Focus visible and trapped in modals
  • Color contrast AA
  • Screen reader labels on icons
  • axe-core CI on critical pages

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Accessibility audit checklist for release..

  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity

8. Semantic versioning for design system.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Breaking: removed prop, token rename → major.
  2. New component → minor.
  3. Bugfix → patch.
  4. Consumers pin ranges; codemods for majors.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Breaking: removed prop, token rename → major. New component → minor. Bugfix → patch. Consumers pin ranges; codemods for majors.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Semantic versioning for design system..

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.

  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
9. Playwright vs Cypress for E2E.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)
  1. Playwright: multi-browser, parallel, auto-wait, better for CI scale.
  2. Cypress: DX, time-travel debug — Chromium-focused historically.
  3. Senior teams often Playwright for cross-browser.

Pro move: tie each point to something observable in DevTools or a metric (TTFB, FCP, INP).

Playwright: multi-browser, parallel, auto-wait, better for CI scale. Cypress: DX, time-travel debug — Chromium-focused historically. Senior teams often Playwright for cross-browser.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Playwright vs Cypress for E2E..

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.

  2. Source of truth for design-dev handoff.

  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
11. SRI Subresource Integrity
Reasoning Hash verify CDN scripts untampered.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Hash verify CDN scripts untampered.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
integrity sha384-... crossorigin anonymous
Trade-off Update hash when vendor updates file.

Security checklist item.

Production example — Walmart

Context: Grocery + pickup — inventory accuracy, store maps, associate offline tools. Relevant when discussing: SRI Subresource Integrity.

Why they chose this approach: Real-time inventory pessimistic at checkout; offline outbox for store apps.

  • Pros: Omnichannel integration
  • Pros: Strong a11y on checkout for all ages
  • Cons: Legacy systems reflect in FE complexity
  • Cons: Third-party analytics impact INP
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
12. npm supply chain audit
Reasoning lockfile CI audit Renovate grouped PRs Socket scanning.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

lockfile CI audit Renovate grouped PRs Socket scanning.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
pnpm audit fail high critical
Trade-off Zero day response process documented.

Senior owns dependency hygiene.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: npm supply chain audit.

Why they chose this approach: Pessimistic payment flows; optimistic cart with server validation gates.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
13. Dependabot vs Renovate
Reasoning Renovate more configurable grouped monorepo schedules.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Renovate more configurable grouped monorepo schedules.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
renovate.json schedule weekly group react
Trade-off Noise fatigue if ungrouped daily PRs.

Pick one org-wide.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Dependabot vs Renovate.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
14. Conventional commits
Reasoning feat fix chore — semantic release changelog automation.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

feat fix chore — semantic release changelog automation.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
feat(cart): add idempotency key header
Trade-off Enforce commitlint husky.

Release engineering awareness.

Production example — Myntra

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Conventional commits.

Why they chose this approach: Aspect-ratio placeholders + lazy below-fold images + SSR on category SEO pages.

  • Pros: Visual commerce polish
  • Pros: Design system for fashion components
  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.
15. Trunk-based vs GitFlow
Reasoning Short-lived branches main always releasable feature flags.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Short-lived branches main always releasable feature flags.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
PR merge within 1-2 days max
Trade-off GitFlow slow for continuous delivery.

Most product companies trunk-based now.

Assert perf scores bundle size on PR.

Example

20. Rate limit 429 UX
Reasoning Retry-After header exponential backoff user message.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Retry-After header exponential backoff user message.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
await sleep(parseRetryAfter(res))
Trade-off Jitter retries prevent thundering herd.

API contract respect.

Production example — Amazon

Context: E-commerce at scale — checkout correctness, search latency, and seller tools reliability over flashy UX. Relevant when discussing: Rate limit 429 UX.

  • Pros: Battle-tested idempotency patterns
  • Pros: Massive A/B infra
  • Cons: Legacy Webpack islands remain
  • Cons: Many teams = inconsistent patterns

21. Cursor vs offset pagination API
Reasoning Cursor stable live datasets; offset breaks when rows inserted mid-list.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Cursor stable live datasets; offset breaks when rows inserted mid-list.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
GET /items?cursor=eyJpZCI6...
Trade-off Offset OK admin small static tables.

Infinite scroll uses cursor.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Cursor vs offset pagination API.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks

22. Idempotent PUT PATCH retries
Reasoning Network fail retry same body safe — server dedupes.

Network fail retry same body safe — server dedupes.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
Retry fetch with same Idempotency-Key
Trade-off GET idempotent by definition POST often not.

Payment flows critical.

Context: Fashion — image-heavy PLP, size filters, wishlist; CLS from product cards is conversion killer. Relevant when discussing: Idempotent PUT PATCH retries.

  • Cons: Huge image bytes vs general ecommerce
  • Cons: Filter state in URL complexity

Legal + perf intersection.

25. Staff meta-skill answer structure
Reasoning Context options decision trade-offs metric — every senior answer.
Interview talking points (expand aloud)

Context options decision trade-offs metric — every senior answer.

Pause after each phase and ask: "Want me to go deeper on network, rendering, or JS execution?"

Example
2-3 minute spoken arc practice daily
Trade-off Judgment over trivia.

This meta-question closes prep guide.

Production example — Flipkart

Context: Mobile-first India traffic — sale events spike 10×; BFF and CDN tuning critical on Jio networks. Relevant when discussing: Staff meta-skill answer structure.

Why they chose this approach: Short TTL on price API during Big Billion Days; preconnect to image CDN.

  • Pros: Mobile web highly optimized
  • Pros: Strong sale-day runbooks
  • Cons: Redirect chain history hurt TTFB until fixed
  • Cons: MFE skew incidents during peaks
Follow-up Q&A (with answers)
  • Attack vector? XSS, CSRF, IDOR, token theft — map control to STRIDE category briefly.
  • Compliance? GDPR consent, SOC2 logging, PCI if payments — only claim what the scenario needs.
  • Incident response? Detect via alert, rollback, comms template, postmortem — show ops maturity not just code.
If the interviewer pushes deeper
  • Give a concrete production example: problem → constraint → decision → measurable outcome.
  • Name one alternative you rejected (e.g. simpler but slower, or faster but harder to hire for) and the deciding factor.
  • State validation: Lighthouse/RUM metric, test type, or Profiler number you would check before calling it done.

System design loop: Start with constraints & assumptions → draw boxes (shell, data, realtime) → name trade-offs → metrics. Use CARTS on every scenario in System Design.

Package managers: Be ready to defend npm vs pnpm with phantom deps, CI speed, and monorepo story — see Package Managers.

Answer format: Reasoning (why) → Example (code) → Trade-off (what you give up) → Tip (what interviewer wants to hear).
